question: A 28-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash on his face that he noticed after spending time outdoors last weekend. He describes the rash as red and itchy, specifically across his cheeks and nose. He also reports fatigue and some stiffness in the morning that eases as the day progresses. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On examination, the rash is erythematous and spares the nasal folds. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the small joints of his hands. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and a positive antinuclear antibody test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 5-day history of joint pain and swelling that started in his ankles and has since spread to his knees and wrists. He reports recently returning from a trip to Central America. He experienced a febrile illness with headache and muscle pain while traveling, but these symptoms resolved spontaneously. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, multiple joints are swollen and tender with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies show mild leukopenia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 54-year-old male presents to his family physician with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past six months. He also notes occasional swelling of his parotid glands, which he describes as uncomfortable but not painful. He denies any changes in vision or difficulty swallowing. His medical history is significant only for mild seasonal allergies. He has no known drug allergies. On examination, the physician observes bilateral parotid gland enlargement. His vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive ANA test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past 3 months. He reports that the stiffness is worse in the mornings and usually lasts over an hour. He also notes occasional fatigue and low-grade fevers. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/h and a positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 74-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe back pain following a minor fall at home. He reports that he tripped over a rug and landed on his buttocks. He denies any loss of consciousness or head trauma. The pain is localized to the mid-thoracic region and worsens with movement or coughing. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, both well-controlled with medication. He denies any history of cancer or chronic corticosteroid use. On examination, he is in moderate discomfort, but there are no neurological deficits. Spinal X-rays reveal a compression fracture at T7. Laboratory results show normal calcium, phosphate, and renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists that have been worsening over the last six months. He reports the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting more than an hour before improving with activity. He denies any history of trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with amlodipine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with persistent pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists that has progressively worsened over the last 6 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes that over-the-counter pain medications provide only minimal relief. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the presence of firm, subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows erosions at the joint margins. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to fatigue and joint pain in his hands and wrists for the past several months. He reports morning stiffness lasting about two hours. His medical history is notable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both of which are well-controlled. He denies any recent travel or infection. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and fatigue. He notes that the stiffness improves as the day progresses. He denies any fevers or recent infections. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, primarily affecting his hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning, lasting for about 1.5 hours, and improves with activity throughout the day. He denies any recent infections or injuries. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about 2 hours and improving as the day progresses. The pain is located in his hands, wrists, and feet symmetrically. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or skin rashes. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints, wrists, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of tightening of the skin on his fingers and episodes of color changes in his fingers upon exposure to cold. He describes the fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red with a throbbing sensation upon rewarming. He also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain. He denies any recent infections or use of new medications. His past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals shiny, tight skin over his fingers and decreased range of motion in the small joints of the hands. There is no swelling or warmth over the joints. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past six months. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for about two hours before improving. On examination, there is notable synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Several small subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of both forearms. The patient has a history of smoking and reports no significant weight loss or fever. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a complaint of fatigue and muscle weakness for the past two months. He also notes swelling in his ankles and occasional headaches. His medical history is significant for hyperlipidemia, for which he takes a statin. He denies tobacco use and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 165/100 mmHg and mild periorbital puffiness. Laboratory tests show a serum creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL (previously 1.0 mg/dL), blood urea nitrogen of 45 mg/dL, and urinary analysis reveals 2+ proteinuria with no red or white blood cells. Serum complement levels are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of swelling and stiffness in his hands and feet for the past two months. He notes that the stiffness is most notable in the morning and improves throughout the day. He reports feeling more fatigued recently and has noticed mild weight loss. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, as well as the metatarsophalangeal joints of both feet. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic with joint pain and swelling in his knees, wrists, and ankles that began abruptly three days ago. His medical history is unremarkable, and he states that he recently returned from a hiking trip to Brazil. He reports experiencing fever and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. On physical examination, he has mild synovitis and significant tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show mild leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. Serologic testing for specific infectious agents is pending. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain that began while he was lifting a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the midline at the level of his lower thoracic spine. He denies any trauma or falls. His medical history is significant for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and long-term glucocorticoid use. He also reports occasional morning stiffness in his hands that lasts about 30 minutes. Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation over the T12 vertebra, with no neurological deficits. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and significant morning stiffness that persists for about 2 hours each day. The symptoms began insidiously over the past several months and have progressively worsened. He reports difficulty in performing daily tasks due to pain and stiffness in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. His medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. He denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight loss. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the aforementioned joints, along with mild ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels, as well as a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of lower back pain and stiffness that improves with exercise but is worse in the morning. He also mentions occasional pain in his hips. He has no significant medical history and does not take any medications. He denies trauma to the back. He has a family history of an uncle with psoriasis. Physical examination shows limited forward flexion of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and CRP. HLA-B27 is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain, which has persisted for several months. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to the pain. His medical history is significant for a positive family history of autoimmune disease. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, and serology is positive for anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 33-year-old man comes to the clinic due to recurring painful oral ulcers and genital sores for the past 2 years. He reports these episodes occurring every few months and they usually resolve in about 10 days without scarring. Additionally, he mentions occasional joint pain in his knees and ankles, which he attributes to his job as a construction worker. He denies any significant weight loss, but he often feels fatigued. On examination, there are shallow ulcers present in the oral cavity and erythematous nodular lesions on the scrotum. The physical exam also reveals tenderness in the right knee without effusion. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated C-reactive protein. Considering these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man visits the clinic due to persistent fatigue and joint pain. He reports feeling generally unwell over the past 6 months, with morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. His joints, particularly the wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands, are symmetrically swollen and tender. He denies any significant trauma or recent infections. His medical history is unremarkable except for mild hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, with a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 68 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. He reports that the pain is present in both his hands and knees, is worse in the morning, and improves after about an hour. He has also noticed swelling and stiffness in these joints. His medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. He denies any recent infections, weight loss, or rashes. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with difficulty walking and a noticeable dragging of his right foot for the past few weeks. He reports occasional tripping and a slapping sound when his right foot touches the ground. The patient works as a carpenter and denies any recent trauma or injury. He mentions mild back pain and tingling sensations in his right leg. On examination, there is a weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot, and the patient has a high-stepping gait. Reflexes are normal, and there is no sensory deficit. MRI of the lumbar spine shows a herniated disc at the L4-L5 level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 6 months. He describes the pain as worsening in the morning and improving throughout the day with activity. He also reports fatigue and occasional fever. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers. He reports that the ulcers in his mouth make it difficult for him to eat, while the genital ulcers are causing him significant discomfort. He also mentions experiencing fatigue and occasional joint pain in his knees and ankles. He denies any recent travel or new sexual partners. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or use alcohol. On examination, there are shallow, well-demarcated ulcers in the oral cavity and several similar ulcers on the scrotum. There is no significant lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 years. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (anti-CCP). Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue and multiple subcutaneous nodules on both elbows and forearms. He mentions that these nodules have developed gradually over the past few months. He also reports frequent episodes of fever and night sweats. On examination, the nodules are firm, non-tender, and located over the extensor surfaces. He has no significant joint pain or swelling, although he occasionally experiences morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but rheumatoid factor is negative. X-rays of the elbows show soft tissue swelling without any joint erosion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of progressive weight gain and swelling around his eyes over the past 4 months. He also reports stiffness in his joints, particularly in the morning, which takes about an hour to improve. He denies significant changes in diet or physical activity. He has a history of hypertension and is on lisinopril. On examination, he has facial puffiness and mild edema in his lower extremities. Laboratory studies show elevated creatinine and proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 32-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of a purplish mottled skin discoloration on his legs and recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in his calves. He reports several episodes of painful blue nodules that have appeared and resolved over the past few months. His past medical history is significant for a deep vein thrombosis 6 months ago, for which he completed a course of anticoagulant therapy. He denies any recent trauma or surgery. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on both lower extremities and tenderness in the calves. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, normal inflammatory markers, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient's condition?

A 42-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a three-month history of fatigue, low-grade fevers, and joint pain in his hands and knees. He also reports the development of firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms, which have gradually increased in size. He denies any significant weight changes or recent infections. On examination, his joints are slightly swollen, and there are multiple subcutaneous nodules palpable at the olecranon processes bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves as the day progresses. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male visits your clinic due to progressive joint deformity and swelling in his hands over the past year. The patient reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, as well as pain in multiple joints including his wrists and fingers. He has a history of smoking and drinks occasionally. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). X-rays demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man visits his primary care clinic due to recurrent episodes of severe pain in his right big toe, which started two years ago. Each episode is characterized by sudden onset of excruciating pain, redness, and swelling, lasting several days before resolving spontaneously. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with medication. He admits to consuming beer regularly over the weekends. On examination, the metatarsophalangeal joint of the right big toe is swollen, erythematous, and tender to touch. Palpable nodules are noted on his elbows and Achilles tendons. A synovial fluid analysis from a previous episode revealed negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about two hours. He reports that the stiffness improves as the day progresses. The pain is primarily in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints, and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of recurrent painful oral ulcers for the past year. He describes similar ulcers on his genital region that have occurred occasionally. In addition to these ulcers, he reports experiencing joint pain, particularly in the knees and ankles, and general fatigue. He has noticed some tenderness over the affected joints but denies any swelling or morning stiffness. He has no history of sexually transmitted infections. Physical examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and tender nodules on the anterior tibial region. His vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein but are negative for rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's presentation?

A 52-year-old man visits his physician due to a 6-month history of progressive hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and noticeable swelling of his finger joints. On examination, there is bilateral synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He reports that two weeks ago he had an upper respiratory tract infection characterized by a sore throat, runny nose, and low-grade fever, which resolved without treatment. Since then, he has been experiencing worsening body aches, particularly affecting his shoulders, back, and knees. He denies any joint swelling, morning stiffness, fever, or rash at this time. His past medical history is unremarkable. He works as a software engineer and is under considerable stress to meet project deadlines. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic reporting chronic neck pain and numbness in both arms for the past 6 months. He describes the pain as a constant dull ache, which sometimes becomes sharp and radiates down his shoulders into his hands. The numbness in his hands worsens with certain neck movements. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch in the C5-C6 dermatomes bilaterally, and Spurling's test elicits pain radiating into the arm. Reflexes are diminished in the biceps bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 43-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of worsening muscle weakness, primarily affecting his shoulders and hips, that has progressively developed over the past 3 months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead, but denies any joint pain. He has experienced mild fatigue but does not report fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, there is symmetric proximal muscle weakness, but no tenderness or swelling of the joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatine kinase level. An electromyography study shows findings consistent with myopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of new-onset pain and swelling in his wrists and hands. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Serum testing is positive for both rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour which improves later in the day. He has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing reveals a positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care provider with a 6-month history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. On examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surface of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. Hand x-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with generalized bone pain and fatigue, which he has been experiencing for the past three months. He reports that the pain is particularly noticeable in his hips and lower back. He denies any history of trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. He consumes alcohol occasionally and denies smoking. On physical examination, there is mild tenderness over the lumbar spine and ribs. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL and an alkaline phosphatase level of 350 U/L. There is no significant change in kidney function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. He has difficulty making a fist in the morning, and his wife has noticed swelling in his knuckles and wrists. His medical history is significant for smoking half a pack of cigarettes daily for the past 20 years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as swelling at the wrists. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 4 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves throughout the day. He denies any recent infections or injuries. His medical history is unremarkable except for mild seasonal allergies. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) is positive. Anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a persistent skin rash and joint pain over the last few months. He reports that his skin often exhibits a net-like purple discoloration, primarily on his legs, which becomes more prominent in cold weather. Additionally, he has experienced episodes of calf pain and swelling that resolve with rest and warm compressions. His medical history is significant for two recent episodes of deep vein thrombosis. He denies smoking or illicit drug use and drinks alcohol occasionally. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. A skin biopsy reveals a non-inflammatory thrombosis in small blood vessels. Given his presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressive joint deformity in his hands over the last year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than one hour, which improves with activity. He has a history of smoking 1 pack per day for 20 years. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-year history of chronic lower back pain. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with exercise. He also has intermittent pain and swelling in his right ankle. He denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. He has a family history of psoriasis in his mother and no known history of autoimmune diseases. He works as a software engineer and does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with difficulty walking and a dragging sensation in his right foot for the past two weeks. He denies any recent trauma or illness. He has a medical history of type 1 diabetes mellitus, which is managed with insulin therapy. He reports feeling fatigued and experiencing intermittent joint pain over the last few months, primarily in the mornings. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot, and he has a high-stepping gait. Sensory examination reveals decreased sensation on the dorsum of the right foot and lateral aspect of the leg. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers and glucose levels. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists, particularly in the morning, for the past four months. He reports the stiffness lasts for more than one hour before improving. He has noticed swelling in his fingers and wrists, and joint pain that is worse in the morning but improves with activity. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His medical history is significant for smoking half a pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, stiffness, and swelling. He reports that the symptoms are worst in the morning and improve throughout the day. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of both forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with left shoulder pain that has worsened over the past 3 months. He describes the pain as a deep ache, aggravated by overhead activities and reaching behind his back. He has difficulty with everyday activities, such as dressing and grooming. His past medical history is significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or systemic symptoms. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a reduced active range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. Passive range of motion is similarly limited. An x-ray of the shoulder shows no acute fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive hand deformities and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He notes that his symptoms started several years ago but have worsened in the last few months. He reports difficulty in performing daily activities such as opening jars and typing. His medical history is significant for hypertension, and he is a former smoker. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers and subluxation of the MCP joints. Laboratory testing shows elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient’s condition?

A 50-year-old man visits his primary care provider with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists over the past 3 months. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving. He has noted similar symptoms symmetrically in both hands. The patient denies any recent infections or trauma. His family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with tenderness. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising. He reports significant swelling and pain in his knees and ankles, and has noticed small red spots on his skin. He denies any recent infections or trauma. He works as a teacher and denies any occupational exposures. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on the lower extremities, and tenderness in the knees and ankles. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,500/mm3, and platelet count of 45,000/mm3. Serum electrolyte levels and renal function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of fever, fatigue, and diffuse joint pain. He reports experiencing night sweats and a 5-pound weight loss over this period. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals tenderness and slight swelling in the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with normal white blood cell counts. Blood cultures are pending. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.F: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question: A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent lower back pain for the past several months. He reports the pain is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity throughout the day. The patient also notes recent difficulty in bending forward and feels stiff for over an hour after waking up. He has no significant past medical history and denies any history of trauma or recent illness. Physical examination reveals limited flexion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the pelvis is ordered and reveals sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that began a week ago. He mentions having traveled to the Caribbean two weeks prior. He describes the joint symptoms as acute in onset, affecting his hands, wrists, knees, and ankles, and denies any previous joint issues. He also reports fever, fatigue, and a rash that has since resolved. Physical examination shows synovitis in multiple joints. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, negative rheumatoid factor, and negative anti-CCP antibodies. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with difficulty walking and a noticeable dragging of the left foot for the past 3 weeks. He reports tripping frequently and struggling to lift his foot while walking. The patient denies any recent trauma or changes in sensation. He does mention some recent episodes of mild lower back pain radiating to the left leg. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the left foot and diminished ankle reflexes. Sensation to light touch and pinprick are intact. Medical history reveals occasional joint pain in the hands, which the patient attributes to long hours typing at his desk job. Laboratory tests show a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal rheumatoid factor levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that have been gradually worsening over the past 6 months. He reports feeling stiff in the mornings, particularly in his hands and wrists, which lasts for about an hour before improving. He denies recent trauma or overuse of his joints. His past medical history is significant for mild hypertension. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe pain, redness, and swelling in his right big toe that started suddenly last night. He describes experiencing similar episodes in the past, typically affecting the same toe, and lasting for a few days before resolving on their own. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He occasionally drinks alcohol and eats red meat. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is warm, swollen, and extremely tender to touch, with a chalky white deposit visible on the overlying skin. Laboratory studies show elevated serum uric acid levels. Synovial fluid analysis from the affected joint reveals needle-shaped crystals under polarized light microscopy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressively worsening joint pain, swelling, and stiffness in his hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is particularly noticeable in the mornings and lasts for over an hour. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent infections or injuries. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that began about two weeks after recovering from an upper respiratory tract infection. He describes the aches as generalized and notes they are worst in the morning, though they do not significantly improve throughout the day. He also reports a low-grade fever and mild sore throat that resolved on its own. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. There is no evidence of joint swelling, erythema, or warmth. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in C-reactive protein and normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient's current condition?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive right shoulder pain and difficulty moving his arm. He describes the pain as a deep, aching sensation that worsens with activity and at night. He denies any recent trauma or overuse. His medical history is notable for well-controlled hypertension. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a significant limitation in active and passive range of motion, particularly with external rotation and abduction. There is no swelling, warmth, or erythema around the shoulder joint. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. X-rays of the shoulder reveal no acute bony abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the last 18 months. He describes the stiffness as being most severe in the morning, lasting for more than an hour, and improving throughout the day. On examination, the patient's metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally. Several firm, subcutaneous nodules are palpated over his elbows. Laboratory findings reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue for the past 6 months. He reports feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and has noticed a 10-pound weight gain despite no changes in his diet or exercise routine. He also mentions occasional joint stiffness in the mornings, primarily affecting his knees and hands, but the stiffness typically resolves within 30 minutes. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, his temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 125/78 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal a serum TSH level of 8.0 mIU/L (normal range 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 level of 0.6 ng/dL (normal range 0.9-1.7 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and a facial rash. He reports that the rash worsens with sun exposure and describes it as red and slightly raised. He also mentions experiencing oral ulcers and generalized fatigue. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash over the malar areas sparing the nasolabial folds. There is tenderness in the small joints of his hands. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test but negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports pain and swelling in his wrists and knees that is worse in the mornings and lasts for more than an hour before improving with movement. He denies any recent trauma or infections but mentions his mother had a similar condition. On physical examination, bilateral swelling and tenderness of the wrists and knees are noted. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and the presence of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man presents to the clinic with chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been worsening over the past two years. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning his shirt. He has noted swelling of his fingers and the appearance of subcutaneous nodules on his elbows. He denies any recent trauma, but his family history is positive for autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces. Laboratory studies reveal positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting for over an hour and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning his shirt. On physical examination, there are multiple swollen, tender metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, with subcutaneous nodules noted on the extensor surfaces of his elbows. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. He describes the joint pain as symmetric and occurring primarily in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and knees. He notices stiffness lasting over an hour in the morning, which improves as the day progresses. He denies any recent infections or trauma. His past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the aforementioned joints, but no deformities are observed. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 87 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 56 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male construction worker presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists that have persisted for the past 8 months. The patient notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. He denies any recent infections or trauma. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, the patient appears generally well but exhibits swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A hand x-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands, wrists, and knees for the past 6 months. He reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. His laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. A hand X-ray demonstrates soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint deformity in his hands and wrists over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in the joints. His family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers and swan neck deformities. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Which is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, he has tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is negative, but rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in multiple small joints of his hands bilaterally. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. There is no history of trauma or recent infections. On exam, his metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Laboratory workup reveals a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent swelling in his neck and armpits. He also reports experiencing unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and intermittent low-grade fevers. He occasionally feels fatigued but attributes this to his demanding job. On examination, multiple non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 1-year history of chronic pain in both hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting several hours, which improves somewhat as the day progresses. He has noticed swelling and tenderness in his joints, along with several firm nodules under the skin on his elbows. He denies fever, weight loss, or recent trauma. He has a family history of autoimmune disease. On physical examination, there is synovitis observed in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports pain in his fingers, ankles, and toes, which is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. He also mentions red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees, which have been present for several years. The patient denies fever, chills, or weight loss but notes occasional fatigue. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. His father has a history of psoriasis. Physical examination reveals dactylitis in several fingers and tender, swollen joints in the distal interphalangeal joints. No rheumatoid nodules are observed. Laboratory results indicate an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands and wrists for the past 6 months. He describes the pain as symmetrical and notes that his hands are stiff for about an hour each morning. He reports no significant past medical history and denies recent infections or trauma. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, that improves with exercise. He denies any history of trauma. The patient also reports episodes of fatigue and decreased appetite. He mentions that over the past few months, he has noticed some difficulty in turning his head and a reduced ability to twist his torso. On physical exam, there is limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the spine shows evidence of sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in both hands, wrists, and knees for the last six months. He notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour which improves as the day progresses. The patient denies any recent infections or travel. He has a history of hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue, generalized joint pain, and occasional fever. He reports feeling excessively tired despite getting adequate sleep and has noticed increased bruising and frequent nosebleeds. He denies any recent travel or unusual exposures. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use recreational drugs. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctiva and mild hepatosplenomegaly. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), pulse is 78/min, blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, White blood cell count 2,500/mm3, Platelet count 85,000/mm3. A peripheral blood smear shows pancytopenia with no abnormal cell populations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic reporting difficulty walking and an inability to lift his right foot. He states that the problem started a week ago and has progressively worsened. He denies recent trauma but recalls a long hike a few days prior to symptom onset. He also complains of occasional low back pain and numbness over the top of his right foot. His medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, he has weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and sensory loss in the area of the first dorsal web space. Reflexes are intact. MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a herniated disc at L4-L5. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about one hour before improving as the day progresses. The joint pain is primarily in his hands, wrists, and feet, affecting both sides of his body symmetrically. He has no history of trauma or recent infections. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of pain and swelling in his right big toe that started suddenly last night. He describes several similar episodes over the past year, each resolving spontaneously over a week. He notes the joint is extremely painful to touch and has been increasingly red and swollen. His past medical history includes obesity and hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He denies any recent trauma or overuse. Physical examination reveals an erythematous, edematous first metatarsophalangeal joint with tenderness on palpation. A similar swelling is noted over the olecranon area, suggestive of tophi. Laboratory studies show an elevated serum uric acid level. Which of the following would most likely be found in the joint fluid analysis from this patient?

options.F: Low C3 and C4 complement levels

question: A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening primarily affecting his fingers and hands over the past few months. He notes that his fingers turn white and painful upon exposure to cold, which is relieved with warmth. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is thickened, tight skin over his fingers and reduced fingertip mobility. Capillary nailfold examination shows dilated capillaries. Laboratory tests reveal a positive anti-centromere antibody, while rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past 6 months. He notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about 1.5 to 2 hours before he feels more mobile. He reports difficulty in performing tasks such as buttoning his shirt and holding a cup. His past medical history includes hypertension and dyslipidemia. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests are positive for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies and rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling that began after a weekend soccer match. He describes the pain as sharp and rates it as an 8 out of 10. He denies any preceding trauma, fever, or chills. He has a history of mild intermittent asthma for which he uses an inhaler as needed. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, and heart rate is 88/min. Physical examination reveals a warm, swollen right knee with limited range of motion and tenderness on palpation. Laboratory tests show:
WBC count 8,500/mm3
ESR 40 mm/hr
CRP 12 mg/L
Serum uric acid 8.5 mg/dL
X-ray of the knee shows mild effusion with no evidence of fracture or dislocation. Aspiration of the knee joint yields cloudy synovial fluid. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of gradually worsening difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He describes significant proximal muscle weakness, but denies any joint pain or stiffness. He reports fatigue and a 10-pound unintentional weight loss over the past few months. He has a history of hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, he has symmetrical proximal muscle weakness in his shoulders and hips, but no joint deformities or swelling. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase and aldolase levels. ANAs are positive, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint pain and deformity over the past two years. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with movement throughout the day. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe back pain after bending down to pick up a newspaper. He describes the pain as sharp and debilitating, located in the mid-thoracic area, and radiating towards the abdomen. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or night sweats. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. He is a former smoker with a 45-pack-year history. On physical examination, the patient is tender to palpation over the thoracic spine and there is a noticeable kyphotic deformity. His vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests reveal a normal white blood cell count, but elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. X-rays of the spine show a wedge-shaped vertebra. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, fatigue, and generalized malaise. He reports night sweats and an unintended weight loss of 5 pounds over the past month. He has mild joint pain, primarily affecting his wrists and knees, but denies significant swelling or morning stiffness. He has no significant past medical history and is not on any regular medications. On examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. His vital signs show a temperature of 100.8°F (38.2°C), blood pressure of 125/80 mmHg, heart rate of 88/min, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. An autoimmune panel shows a negative rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibody (ANA). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Perform a positron emission tomography (PET) scan

question: A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He describes the pain as occurring symmetrically in his wrists, knees, and hands, particularly in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving. He denies any recent infections, rashes, or new medications. He works as an accountant and has no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in his hands and feet. He also reports occasional joint pain and a general feeling of fatigue. He denies any recent infections or significant weight changes. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. He occasionally drinks alcohol but denies smoking or drug use. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Blood tests show hemoglobin A1c of 7.8%. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the presence of firm subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). An X-ray of the hands reveals periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressively worsening pain and deformity in his hands over the past 5 years. He notes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily activities such as buttoning his shirt. His past medical history is notable for hypertension, for which he is taking lisinopril. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, subcutaneous nodules over the elbows, and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and reports decreased grip strength. On examination, there is symmetric synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. His medical history includes hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) are elevated. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a rash and dark-colored urine for the past three days. He notes that the rash began on his lower legs and has slowly spread upwards. He also reports mild joint pain in his knees and ankles, but denies fever, sore throat, or any recent infections. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. On physical examination, he has palpable purpura on his lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal a creatinine level of 1.5 mg/dL and a urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. His complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are otherwise normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, swelling, and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. The patient has noted subcutaneous nodules on his elbows. He reports difficulty performing tasks that require fine motor skills due to pain. He denies any recent trauma or infections. Physical examination reveals tender and swollen metacarpophalangeal joints and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He describes the pain as affecting his wrists, knees, and ankles symmetrically. Morning stiffness lasts over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. He reports no history of trauma. His past medical history is significant for hypertension controlled on lisinopril. On examination, he has swelling and tenderness in the wrists and small joints of the hands. Laboratory studies reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Testing for rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists. He reports that the pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for several hours, and improves with activity. Physical examination reveals palpable synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with several subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain. He reports that the pain is symmetric and involves both his hands and wrists. He mentions experiencing stiffness in the morning that lasts about 1.5 hours before he feels better. Upon examination, you note swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory testing shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. The hand x-ray reveals periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his fingers and wrists for the past six months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour. He also notes swelling in several joints and difficulty with fine motor tasks. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue for the past 6 months. He also notes mild weight gain, cold intolerance, and occasional joint discomfort in his knees and wrists. On physical examination, the patient has dry skin and a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal the following:

Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH): 12 mIU/L
Free thyroxine (T4): 0.7 ng/dL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 30 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor (RF): Negative

Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 2-year history of progressive joint pain and deformity, particularly in his hands and wrists. He states the pain is worse in the mornings and improves as the day progresses. He has a history of smoking but quit 5 years ago. He takes ibuprofen occasionally for the pain. Physical examination shows ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and high levels of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with a 6-month history of progressive difficulty walking and lower extremity weakness. He noticed that his legs feel heavier, and he has to concentrate more on moving them while walking. He denies any trauma or acute injury. He has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. He works as a software engineer and denies tobacco use, but consumes alcohol socially. On physical examination, his strength is 4/5 in both lower extremities with increased muscle tone. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk, and there is also a positive Babinski sign bilaterally. Sensation is intact to light touch and pinprick. MRI of the brain and cervical spine shows demyelination in the cervical cord. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of stiffness and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past 3 months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and lasts for approximately two hours. He denies any history of trauma or recent infections. Upon physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased grip strength noted. There is no erythema or warmth over the affected joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a three-month history of painless swelling of his neck and persistent fatigue. He has also experienced a 10-pound weight loss over the past three months despite a normal appetite. He reports occasional night sweats but denies fever, chills, or recent infections. His medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there are multiple palpable lymph nodes in the cervical region. These nodes are firm, non-tender, and mobile. Laboratory tests reveal elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and a normocytic anemia. A biopsy of one of the lymph nodes is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of symmetric swelling and pain in both hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, improving with activity. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally.
