Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Hand x-rays show marginal erosions at the joint spaces. What is the most appropriate initial management for this patient?

A 24-year-old white man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, predominantly affecting his wrists and knees, along with a facial rash that becomes more pronounced after sun exposure. He also reports intermittent fatigue and mild fever. He denies any recent travel, and his family history is unremarkable for autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals a malar rash and tenderness in the affected joints without significant swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated ANA titers and a mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain, particularly in his wrists, knees, and hands. He reports that the joint pain has been ongoing for several months and is worse in the mornings, often lasting for over an hour before improving somewhat as the day progresses. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both of which are well controlled. A physical exam reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a normocytic anemia pattern, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists that have developed over the past three months. He notes that the symptoms are worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour before improving. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling affecting his wrists, knees, and ankles. He states that the pain started shortly after returning from a trip to the Caribbean. He experienced a high fever and rash during his travel, which resolved spontaneously. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, the affected joints are erythematous and tender with a decreased range of motion. There is no rash or lymphadenopathy noted on the current exam. Laboratory tests reveal mildly elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists, which started a month ago. He mentions morning stiffness lasting about two hours. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications regularly. He works as an accountant and denies any recent trauma. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of headaches and fatigue over the past few months. He has a known history of obesity and mild hyperlipidemia. Recently, he noted swelling in his ankles and an increase in weight, despite no changes to his diet or activity level. He denies any history of diabetes or cardiovascular diseases. His blood pressure is measured at 160/95 mmHg, and laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. Urinalysis shows proteinuria. He reports some stiffness in his fingers in the morning that resolves quickly after waking up; however, he does not have significant joint pain or swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 23-year-old man presents to the clinic with persistent lower back pain for the past six months. He describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving with activity. He notes that he has been waking up during the night due to back discomfort. He denies any recent trauma or injury to the area. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. The patient demonstrates limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a new diagnosis of hypertension. He reports feeling fatigued and having mild joint pain over the past several months. His blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. A urinalysis shows hematuria and proteinuria. The patient denies any history of diabetes or cardiovascular disease and reports taking no medications. Physical examination reveals no significant abnormalities, and his temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F). Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of his condition?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both his hands and wrists for the past two months. He describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. He also reports early morning stiffness lasting over an hour. The patient has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. His family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serological tests are positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and noticeable swelling of the joints, which improves somewhat with activity throughout the day. He also notes the presence of firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. His medical history is significant for controlled hypertension. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes, dry mouth, and swelling in the region of his cheeks for the past several months. He reports intermittent joint pain and fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. He has a history of hypertension, controlled with medication, and is a non-smoker. On physical examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands, and his oral mucosa appears dry. Schirmer's test is positive, indicating reduced tear production. His laboratory results show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of skin changes and joint pain that have been worsening over the past 6 months. He describes his fingers turning white and then blue in response to cold temperatures or stress, followed by redness and tingling as they warm up. He also reports a feeling of tightness across his fingers and difficulty in flexing them. The patient has been experiencing fatigue and occasional joint pain in his hands and wrists but denies any significant morning stiffness. His medical history is unremarkable except for a smoking habit he quit last year. Physical examination reveals shiny, tight skin over the fingers with some telangiectasias on the face. The capillary nailfold examination shows dilated capillaries. Laboratory tests show mildly elevated inflammatory markers, but no significant serologic evidence of rheumatoid arthritis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past six months. He describes the pain as symmetric, affecting both his hands and knees, and notes morning stiffness lasting more than one hour. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, his metacarpophalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, hematocrit 34%, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 65 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man visits his physician due to fatigue and joint pain. He reports that his symptoms, including stiffness and swelling in his hands and wrists, have progressively worsened over the past 6 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and generally improves throughout the day. He denies any recent infections or trauma. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is a non-smoker. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure is 125/82 mm Hg, and pulse is 78 beats per minute. Physical examination reveals tender and swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL (reference range: 13.8-17.2), indicating normocytic anemia, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 68 mm/hr (reference range: <20), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past four months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting approximately two hours before improving as the day progresses. He denies any recent trauma or fever but notes occasional fatigue. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory findings reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in both of his hands and wrists that started a few months ago and has progressively worsened. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and significant swelling in his fingers and wrists. He denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. His medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serological testing is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports that the pain started in his fingers and wrists and is worse in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving slightly as the day goes on. He denies any recent trauma or significant illness. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-ray of the hands shows soft tissue swelling and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old man comes to the office with complaints of acute knee pain and swelling after playing a basketball game. He recalls landing awkwardly after a jump and felt a popping sensation in his right knee. Since then, he has had difficulty bearing weight on the leg. On examination, there is significant swelling and tenderness over the right knee, limited range of motion, and a positive Lachman test. There is no fever, and his other joints are unaffected. An X-ray of the knee shows no fracture. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to the appearance of a rash on his legs and some blood in his urine. The rash started a week ago and is characterized by small, non-blanching, purplish spots mostly on the lower extremities. He also reports joint pain and abdominal discomfort. There is no significant past medical history. On examination, his temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory findings reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising. He reports experiencing swelling and pain in his wrists and knees, which he initially attributed to overuse at work. The patient also mentions feeling more tired lately and has noticed small red spots on his arms. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. He denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, petechiae are noted on his arms and legs, and mild tenderness is present in his wrists and knees. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, WBC of 2,500/mm³, and platelets of 80,000/mm³. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands and feet for the past year. The pain is worse in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. The patient has a history of smoking one pack per day for 20 years but quit 5 years ago. He denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 78 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor test. A hand x-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, swelling, and stiffness in his hands and wrists that have progressively worsened over the past six months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions in the joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his rheumatologist with worsening pain and swelling of his hands and wrists over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and bilateral wrist joints. Laboratory findings reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He reports that over the past several months he has experienced morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and symmetrical swelling in his wrists and fingers. He also mentions persistent fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, treated with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 85 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of right shoulder pain that has progressively worsened over the past three months, along with a reduced range of motion. He describes the pain as a dull ache that occasionally becomes sharp with certain movements, particularly when reaching overhead. He denies any history of trauma or significant past medical history. The patient has no morning stiffness, but experiences difficulty sleeping on his right side due to discomfort. He is a retired office worker who occasionally plays recreational tennis. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder, and passive external rotation is notably limited compared to the opposite side. The rest of the physical exam is unremarkable. An X-ray of the shoulder is performed and shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint deformities in his hands and wrists over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting about two hours and significant difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. His medical history is significant for hypertension, and he currently takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is notable ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling around the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of morning stiffness in his hands and wrists that lasts for more than an hour and improves throughout the day. He has noticed swelling and pain in these joints for the past 6 months. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the bilateral metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. He has several subcutaneous nodules on his elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions in the affected joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 4 months. He reports that the pain is primarily in his wrists, knees, and fingers and that it is worse in the morning, improving slightly as the day progresses. He also notes swelling in these joints. He denies any recent travel or history of trauma. His past medical history includes hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 10-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his parents due to a rash on his legs and blood in his urine. The rash appeared three days ago and consists of small, non-blanching red spots predominantly on the lower extremities. The parents also report that he has been complaining of abdominal pain and joint aches, but they have not noticed any recent fever. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. Laboratory tests reveal normal platelet count, elevated serum creatinine, and urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to his primary care clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain affecting both his hands and wrists. He describes significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour and states that he often feels unusually fatigued. His past medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows erosions at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to pain in his wrists, knees, and ankles. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as bilateral knee effusions. Laboratory evaluation shows a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 70 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with a persistent low-grade fever over the past four weeks. He reports feeling fatigued and has experienced unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds during this period. He also notices occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. His past medical history is significant for hyperlipidemia, and he takes atorvastatin daily. On examination, his temperature is 100.4°F (38.0°C), blood pressure is 130/78 mmHg, pulse is 84/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/h and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels of 20 mg/L. Blood cultures are pending. Chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past three months. He reports that the pain is worse at night and is not relieved by rest or common analgesics. He also notes increased thirst and urination. He denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. His medical history is significant for a kidney stone episode one year ago. On examination, there is mild tenderness over the sternum and ribs. Laboratory studies reveal a calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and an alkaline phosphatase level that is slightly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a fever of 102°F (38.9°C) that has persisted for over two weeks without an identifiable source. He reports fatigue, night sweats, and a 10-pound weight loss over the past month. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled. On physical examination, he has diffuse joint tenderness without significant swelling. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures and initial infectious workup are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.C: Obtain a computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis
options.E: Refer to rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue, joint pain, and recurrent nosebleeds. He reports having difficulty getting out of bed due to generalized weakness and morning stiffness in his hands and wrists that improves as the day progresses. He denies any significant bleeding tendencies in the past. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications regularly. Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on the lower extremities, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,800/mm3, and platelet count of 85,000/mm3. His erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Bone marrow biopsy reveals a hypocellular marrow with fatty infiltration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever for the past two weeks. He recently recovered from a viral upper respiratory infection. He denies any significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness in the joints but no swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 4-month history of fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold even in warm environments. He reports joint pain in his hands and knees, which he has attributed to aging. He denies morning stiffness. He has a history of hyperlipidemia managed with atorvastatin. On examination, his heart rate is 58/min, blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, and temperature is 36.2°C (97.2°F). He appears pale and has dry, coarse skin. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 13 g/dL, TSH of 18 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L), and free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL (normal: 0.9-1.7 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He describes morning stiffness lasting about an hour and notes that his symptoms improve throughout the day. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 14-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a rash and joint pain for the past week. His mother reports that he had a sore throat about two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. The rash is described as purple spots on his lower legs. He also complains of abdominal pain and dark-colored urine. On examination, there is palpable purpura on his buttocks and lower extremities. His joints are tender with a reduced range of motion in the knees. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and proteinuria, but no significant elevation in rheumatoid factor or anti-nuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of acute pain and swelling in his right big toe over the past year. He describes the pain as severe and notes that it often occurs suddenly, especially after consuming red meat and alcohol. Over the last few months, he has also noticed the development of small, firm nodules around his toe and elbow. He is otherwise healthy and takes no regular medications. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is red, warm, and tender with visible tophi. Serum analysis reveals elevated uric acid levels. Synovial fluid analysis shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He describes the stiffness as most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour, and primarily affecting his fingers and wrists. He also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms and elbows that he noticed over the last few weeks. He experiences intermittent low-grade fever and occasional night sweats. He denies any significant pain in his joints, though he mentions mild stiffness in the morning that improves throughout the day. His medical history includes hypertension managed with amlodipine. He denies tobacco or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals firm, non-tender nodules over the extensor surfaces and a mildly enlarged, non-tender liver. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with painful nodules on his shins and joint pain in his ankles and knees that began two weeks ago. He reports that the nodules are tender, and he has been feeling fatigued. He denies any recent trauma or injury. The patient has a history of recurrent sore throats but has no significant past medical history otherwise. On examination, there are multiple erythematous, tender nodules on both shins. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, but rheumatoid factor is negative. A chest x-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man visits the rheumatology clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling. He describes the pain as symmetric, affecting his wrists and fingers predominantly, with morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. He has also noticed fatigue and unintentional weight loss. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is positive, and rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are present. A radiograph of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following best accounts for this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. He notes that the pain is most pronounced in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and knees. The symptoms are worse in the morning and improve with activity throughout the day. He has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the affected joints with a reduced range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, white blood cell count of 8,500/mm3, platelet count of 320,000/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 75 mm/hr. Imaging of his wrists shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and increasing difficulty in performing daily tasks. His medical history is unremarkable except for a minor, self-limiting episode of knee swelling that occurred last year. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally, and several firm, subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, Leukocyte count 7300/mm3, Platelet count 210,000/mm3, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores. He reports that these episodes occur every few weeks and are accompanied by joint pain affecting his knees and ankles. He denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. He has a history of frequent mouth ulcers since his teenage years, but the genital ulcers started in the last year. He is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms irregularly. On examination, multiple shallow ulcers are observed on the buccal mucosa, and there is evidence of healed ulcers on the scrotum. His joints are tender but without significant swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of worsening bilateral hip pain over the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that is exacerbated by prolonged standing and walking. The patient denies any history of trauma or specific injury to the hips. He has noticed some morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes. He works in a construction job, which requires significant physical activity, and has had to take more frequent breaks due to the discomfort. His medical history is significant for obesity and controlled hypertension. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips with pain on internal rotation. Laboratory results show normal inflammatory markers. A plain radiograph of the pelvis reveals joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in the hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 23-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of low back pain. He describes the pain as aching in nature, primarily located in the lower back and buttocks, and notes stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. The patient reports occasional pain in his knees and ankles, along with fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic because of painful joints and a rash that appeared a week ago. He reports that the joint pain started shortly after he removed an engorged tick from his right leg following a camping trip in a wooded area two weeks ago. He has noticed swelling and stiffness in his knees and wrists, particularly in the morning. On examination, he has a temperature of 38.4°C (101.1°F), a bull's-eye rash on his right thigh, and tenderness over the joints mentioned. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Laboratory tests show a high erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) but negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive swelling and pain in his fingers and wrists over the past six months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and significant difficulty with daily activities, such as opening jars and typing at his job. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody levels. X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient's condition?

A 36-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of tightening of the skin on his fingers and color changes in his fingertips upon exposure to cold. He describes that his fingers turn white, then blue, followed by red as they warm up. Over the past six months, he has also experienced difficulty swallowing solids and episodes of heartburn. He reports joint stiffness, particularly in the morning, but denies any significant swelling or pain in the joints. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there is thickening of the skin on his fingers and telangiectasia on his face. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody test with a speckled pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint pain and swelling over the past year. He notes that his symptoms are most severe in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour. He has noticed deformities in his fingers and occasional fevers. There is no history of trauma. On physical exam, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 10-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules over his elbows. The patient has a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A hand x-ray reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He reports that these symptoms began following a recent upper respiratory infection that lasted about five days. He denies any joint swelling or stiffness, and has no significant past medical history. On examination, he appears tired but is in no acute distress. There are no rashes or joint abnormalities. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, mild elevation in C-reactive protein, and normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's current symptoms?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving throughout the day. He has mild swelling in several finger joints bilaterally and reports occasional fatigue. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications regularly. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA). Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of gradually worsening lower back pain and stiffness. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity. The stiffness usually lasts for more than an hour after waking up. He has also experienced intermittent pain in his hips and shoulders. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. His laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with acute pain and swelling in multiple joints, including his wrists, knees, and ankles. He returned from a trip to Southeast Asia two weeks ago. He mentions feeling fatigued and has had a low-grade fever for the past three days. He denies any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, the affected joints are warm and tender with some limitation of movement. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old man visits the clinic with complaints of joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain in his hands, wrists, and knees. He notes that the stiffness improves with activity throughout the day. He denies any fevers or weight loss. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient’s condition?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with painful, blue-tinged lace-like patterns on his skin, primarily on his legs and arms. He reports a history of recurrent episodes of leg swelling and pain, which he attributes to 'bad circulation.' He has had two previous episodes of deep vein thrombosis in the past three years. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable, with no known family history of clotting disorders. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers but normal renal function. Antinuclear antibody test is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to pain in his hands and knees. His medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 86 fL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is markedly elevated. X-rays of the hands show narrowing of the joint spaces and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his rheumatologist with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been worsening over the past year. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has noticed swelling in his wrists and fingers. He also reports small, firm nodules under the skin of his elbows. His medical history includes smoking half a pack of cigarettes daily for the past 30 years. There is a family history of autoimmune disorders. On physical examination, there is evidence of synovitis in multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and high levels of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent fevers and generalized fatigue over the past two months. He mentions that he has also noticed some swelling in his neck and painless lumps in his armpits. He denies any significant joint pain, but occasionally experiences stiffness in the morning that resolves by midday. His past medical history includes an episode of infectious mononucleosis at age 18. On physical examination, he has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions, but no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to persistent joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past three months. He describes the pain as an 8/10 in severity, worse in the mornings, and associated with stiffness that improves as the day progresses. His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies recent trauma or infections. Physical examination reveals synovitis with tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Hand X-rays demonstrate juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past six months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for about two hours before improving slightly throughout the day. His medical history is significant for a family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset pain and swelling in the right big toe. He reports similar episodes over the past year, primarily affecting the same joint, which resolve after a few days without intervention. On examination, the toe is red, warm, and exquisitely tender to touch. He has a history of hypertension and obesity. He denies any recent trauma or fever. Synovial fluid analysis reveals needle-shaped crystals under polarized light. Serum uric acid is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute back pain after lifting a heavy box at home. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back, with no radiation down the legs. He has a history of chronic back pain but notes that this pain feels different and more intense. His medical history is significant for hypertension and a previous hip fracture 3 years ago. He is a former smoker and drinks alcohol socially. On examination, tenderness is noted over the thoracic spine, and he has limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. X-rays of the spine show a compression fracture of the T8 vertebra with decreased vertebral height. Laboratory results reveal normal calcium and phosphate levels, but decreased bone mineral density on a recent DEXA scan. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Order an MRI of the spine

question: A 50-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past year. He has also noticed swelling in the area around his jaw, which has been increasingly noticeable and mildly uncomfortable. He denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement, and the oral mucosa appears dry. A Schirmer test shows reduced tear production bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal positive anti-Ro/SSA antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past three months. He has also noticed a slight yellowing of his skin and mucous membranes. He denies any recent changes in appetite or bowel habits. His medical history is notable only for mild osteoarthritis. On physical examination, the patient appears pale and jaundiced. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic anemia with a low reticulocyte count. Further workup reveals elevated indirect bilirubin levels and the presence of fragmented red blood cells on a peripheral blood smear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain that has persisted for over six months. He reports the pain is particularly severe in the morning and is often accompanied by significant fatigue and sleep disturbances. Despite sleeping for 8 hours, he frequently wakes up feeling unrefreshed. He notes that he has had frequent headaches and experiences a constant 'foggy' feeling. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers and negative rheumatoid factor. Physical examination shows multiple tender points across his body but no swollen joints. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 75-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe back pain after bending over to pick up a newspaper. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back region. He denies any trauma or falls. His past medical history is significant for osteoporosis and hypertension. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history but quit smoking 10 years ago. His current medications include alendronate and amlodipine. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the thoracic spine with no neurological deficits observed. Laboratory tests reveal normal calcium levels and slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint pain and swelling over the past year. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves throughout the day with activity. His symptoms initially began in his wrists and have progressively involved the small joints of his hands and feet. He denies any skin rashes or gastrointestinal symptoms. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests indicate elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A radiograph of his hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old male presents to the clinic with chronic lower back pain that has progressively worsened over the past year. He describes the pain as being most severe in the morning, with stiffness lasting for more than an hour and improving with physical activity. He denies any recent trauma or infection. His medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited spinal flexion. The Schober test is positive. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographs of the spine demonstrate sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that started in his hands and wrists two months ago. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks like opening jars. His past medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with medication. On examination, he has symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with mild warmth. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and feet. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty in performing daily activities. His past medical history includes hypertension, which is well-controlled. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation, swan-neck deformities of the fingers, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. X-rays of the hands and feet reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and pain in his joints for the last few months. He notes that the pain is worse in the mornings and is associated with stiffness that lasts for over an hour. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with a facial rash that he says worsens with sun exposure. He describes the rash as starting on his cheeks and extending across the bridge of his nose, with sparing of the nasolabial folds. Over the past few months, he has also experienced intermittent joint pain, particularly in his wrists and knees, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. Besides these symptoms, he reports occasional fevers and fatigue. On examination, there is a well-demarcated erythematous rash over his malar area and tenderness in multiple joints without significant synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent deep vein thrombosis and skin changes on his legs. He describes a mottled, purplish discoloration on his skin that seems to worsen with cold exposure. He also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable except for similar episodes of thrombosis in the past, which resolved with treatment. Laboratory tests show normal CBC, but elevated inflammatory markers. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on his lower extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.D: Venous Doppler ultrasound of the legs

question: A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past six months. He notes that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting about an hour before improving somewhat as the day progresses. His past medical history includes mild hypertension, managed with lifestyle modifications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a chief complaint of progressive deformity of his hands and wrists over the past 5 years. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves with activity. He has a history of episodes of joint pain and swelling but has not sought medical attention until now. On physical examination, there are noticeable ulnar deviations of the fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints and swan-neck deformities of several fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated levels of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint erosions and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain affecting both hands and wrists over the past six months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He has no significant medical history and denies smoking or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) at 45 mm/hr, and negative rheumatoid factor (RF). An X-ray of the hands shows narrowing of the joint spaces and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty gripping objects. On examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, and subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surface of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) titers. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and significant fatigue. He also reports swelling and tenderness in his neck and armpits. He denies any recent travel or known sick contacts. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On physical examination, he has multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and anemia. A chest x-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old man visits the clinic with complaints of lower back pain that has been present for the last six months. He describes the pain as dull and achy, with stiffness that is worse in the mornings and improves with activity throughout the day. He has difficulty sitting for long periods and mentions occasional pain in his buttocks. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and reduced spinal flexion. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. The pain and stiffness improve with activity throughout the day. He also reports noticing firm lumps under the skin near his elbows. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past several months. He describes the pain as symmetric, affecting his wrists and knees, and it is associated with stiffness lasting more than an hour each morning. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, tenderness and swelling are noted in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent lymphadenopathy. He notes that the swelling is most pronounced in his neck and underarms. The patient also reports night sweats, unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds, and intermittent low-grade fevers over the past several weeks. He denies any joint pain, rashes, or recent infections. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Physical examination reveals palpable, firm lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels. A lymph node biopsy is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about 1.5 hours, and affects both hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically. The patient denies any trauma or recent infections. He has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are also elevated. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man visits his physician due to fatigue and joint pain. He reports stiffness in his fingers, wrists, and knees, particularly in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including his mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the small joints of his hands, wrists, and knees. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 38-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with concerns about red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees that have been present for the past 6 months. He reports that these lesions occasionally itch but have become more bothersome due to their appearance. He also describes new-onset joint pain in his fingers and toes, which he notes is asymmetric and sometimes leads to swelling. He denies any morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes. The patient has a family history of psoriasis in his mother. Physical examination reveals well-defined, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs, as well as dactylitis in a few digits. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of symmetric joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He reports difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning his shirt due to pain and swelling in his hands. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most specific antibody associated with his most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with chronic bilateral pain in his hands and wrists, which he has experienced for over a year. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. Physical examination reveals synovitis and multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Order anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody testing
options.G: Monitor symptoms with follow-up in 6 months

question: A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive difficulty in climbing stairs and rising from a seated position over the past several months. He denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. He has also noticed difficulty lifting objects above his head. He has a history of hypertension, controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of both the upper and lower extremities, with no tenderness, swelling, or deformities in the joints. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels, while erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is mildly elevated. A recent electromyography study shows findings consistent with an inflammatory myopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden, severe pain and swelling in his right big toe that started overnight. He reports similar episodes in the past, which have been increasing in frequency. He denies any trauma but admits to a diet high in red meat and seafood. He also consumes alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals a swollen, red, and tender metatarsophalangeal joint with visible tophi on the helix of his left ear. Laboratory tests show elevated white blood cell count and uric acid level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling, which have been progressively worsening over the past two years. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than one hour and difficulty gripping objects. His past medical history is notable for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are tender and swollen bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this condition?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He describes the pain as worse in the morning and affecting his wrists and fingers bilaterally. He has noticed that the stiffness lasts more than an hour after waking up. He denies any recent infections or fevers. His medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing shows a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic due to intermittent fever and fatigue for the last 3 months. He also reports the development of painless lumps under the skin on his elbows and forearms. He has a history of treating recurrent sinus infections and ulcerative colitis. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms, which are nontender and mobile. His lungs are clear to auscultation, and there is no joint swelling or tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, and serology is notable for a positive antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) test. A biopsy of one of the subcutaneous nodules shows granulomatous inflammation with necrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his wrists and fingers for the past 6 months. The stiffness is more pronounced in the morning and lasts for about an hour. He notes that his symptoms improve as the day progresses. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that began three months ago. He describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before improving as the day progresses. He has noticed swelling symmetrically in his fingers and wrists. He denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. His medical history is significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with complaints of joint pain that began in his knees and has moved to his elbows and wrists over the past week. He also reports a sore throat two weeks ago, which he initially attributed to a mild cold. He has noticed some fatigue but denies any fever, weight loss, or rash. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On examination, he is afebrile, and there is mild swelling and tenderness over the affected joints without erythema. A rapid strep test is negative. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. He also reports experiencing joint pain in his knees and elbows, which he describes as aching and persistent but not severe. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy but no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP levels. A chest X-ray performed as part of the initial workup is unremarkable. The patient denies any recent travel history, weight loss, or exposure to sick contacts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers that have been occurring for the past six months. He reports that the ulcers tend to heal and then recur after a few weeks. Additionally, he has experienced some joint pain and stiffness in his knees and ankles, which is usually worse in the morning but improves throughout the day. He also mentions occasional skin lesions on his lower legs. Laboratory results show elevated C-reactive protein and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain. He reports experiencing morning stiffness and pain in his hands and knees for several months. These symptoms improve as the day progresses. His medical history includes treated hyperlipidemia and he takes no other medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man visits his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. The joint pain is mainly in his wrists, knees, and ankles and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. He has a history of smoking and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a normal mean corpuscular volume, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue and joint pain. The joint pain is described as symmetric and affects his wrists, knees, and ankles. He reports morning stiffness lasting about 2 hours. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma or infections. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, but no deformities. Laboratory results reveal hemoglobin of 11 g/dL, hematocrit of 33%, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with new-onset stiffness and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning, lasting about two hours, and improve with activity throughout the day. He denies any recent trauma or infections. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. He describes morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour and reports swelling in his hands and wrists. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a rheumatoid factor (RF) level of 80 IU/mL. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain, fatigue, and recent unintentional weight loss. He reports that the bone pain is diffuse but has been particularly bothersome in his lower back and ribs. Laboratory workup reveals a serum calcium level of 12.2 mg/dL, serum albumin of 4.0 g/dL, and serum phosphorus of 2.3 mg/dL. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is controlled with medication. He denies recent trauma or changes in physical activity. Physical examination shows mild tenderness to palpation over the lower spine. Further evaluation reveals a serum parathyroid hormone (PTH) level that is significantly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 5-year history of chronic hand and wrist pain. He reports stiffness in the morning lasting about 2 hours, which improves throughout the day. Over the past year, he has noticed swelling in his wrists and fingers, and the development of firm nodules over the extensor surfaces of his forearms. His medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is synovitis involving the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with associated subcutaneous nodules. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show juxta-articular erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past 8 months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before he feels comfortable moving his hands. His symptoms have been progressively worsening, and he has difficulty performing daily tasks that require fine motor skills. He denies any recent injuries or known allergies. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and a cough productive of yellowish sputum. He also reports fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. His past medical history is significant for hypertension. On examination, he has bilateral crackles in the lung bases and mild edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatinine level and active urinary sediment with red blood cell casts. A chest X-ray shows bilateral nodular infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recent episodes of painful, purple, mottled skin discoloration on his legs that worsen with cold exposure. He also reports several episodes of leg swelling and warmth in the past year, which resolved with anticoagulation. His medical history is significant for hypertension and a previous deep vein thrombosis (DVT). On examination, he has livedo reticularis on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a positive lupus anticoagulant and elevated anticardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
