A 55-year-old male presents to the emergency department with complaints of persistent bone pain, particularly in his back and ribs, that has been progressively worsening over the past few months. He also notes feeling increasingly fatigued and has experienced some unintentional weight loss. His medical history is significant for a renal stone episode five years ago. He states that he occasionally feels nauseous and has had episodes of constipation. Laboratory tests reveal a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL (normal range: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and a parathyroid hormone (PTH) level that is elevated. His complete blood count, renal function tests, and liver function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with this patient's presentation?

A 45-year-old man comes to the emergency department after tripping and landing on his outstretched hand. He complains of significant pain in his right wrist and has difficulty moving it. He denies any other injuries or significant past medical history. He does not take any medications regularly and has no known allergies. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the distal radius with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no deformity noted in other joints. His vital signs are stable: temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. An X-ray of the wrist shows a distal radius fracture with mild displacement. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 34-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling over the past six months. He specifically notes pain in his right knee, left ankle, and a few fingers on his right hand. He also reports that he has noticed red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees that occasionally itch. He has no significant past medical history and no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals dactylitis in the right index finger and well-demarcated erythematous plaques with overlying silvery scales on his skin. Laboratory studies show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue. He reports feeling tired all day despite getting 8 hours of sleep each night and has gained 10 pounds over the last two months despite no changes to his diet. He also describes episodes of feeling cold, particularly in the evenings, and mentions occasional joint pain in his knees and wrists, which he attributes to his job as a warehouse worker. He has no significant medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, he appears pale, and his skin is dry. Initial laboratory tests reveal a TSH level of 8.5 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 level of 0.8 ng/dL (normal 0.9-1.7 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his physician with fatigue and joint pain over the past 3 months. He reports the pain is primarily in his elbows and knees. On examination, multiple subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of his forearms. He also has a low-grade fever and has lost 5 kg (11 lbs) unintentionally in the past two months. Laboratory studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers and anemia of chronic disease. A chest X-ray indicates interstitial lung disease. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given this patient's systemic symptoms?

A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with joint pain and a new rash. He reports that two weeks ago he noticed a red circular rash with central clearing on his left thigh, which has since resolved. He now experiences pain and swelling in his knees and wrists. He denies any recent travel history. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, the patient has a temperature of 38.1°C (100.6°F). There are no visible skin lesions currently present. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe low back pain. He describes the pain as sharp and debilitating, making it difficult to stand or walk. He does not recall any specific injury. He has a history of chronic corticosteroid use for polymyalgia rheumatica. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the lower thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits are noted. His temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal normal calcium and phosphate levels, but a significantly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). X-ray of the thoracolumbar spine shows a compressed vertebral body. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic because of persistent bone pain in his legs and lower back for the past 3 months. He reports fatigue and occasional headaches. His medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the tibia and lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show a calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL) and a serum alkaline phosphatase level of 160 U/L (normal: 44-147 U/L). Serum phosphate and parathyroid hormone levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with unexplained weight gain, notable periorbital edema, and joint stiffness primarily in the morning for the past few months. He denies any changes in diet or exercise but mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing mild muscle cramps. His past medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is puffiness around the eyes and mild edema of the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show normal blood glucose, elevated creatinine, hypoalbuminemia, and 4+ proteinuria on urinalysis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive swelling and pain in his hand joints over the past 8 months. He notes morning stiffness that lasts for more than an hour, improving with activity. He has difficulty holding objects and performing tasks due to deformities in his hands. On examination, there is notable swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe pain in his right wrist after slipping on ice and falling on an outstretched hand. He reports significant swelling and difficulty moving the wrist. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and significant swelling. An X-ray of the wrist shows a fracture of the distal radius. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any previous joint issues or chronic illnesses. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.C: Prescribe a short course of oral corticosteroids
options.D: Refer for an orthopedic consultation for possible surgical intervention
options.F: Perform an MRI of the wrist

question: A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. The pain is described as a symmetrical polyarthritis affecting his wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and has difficulty performing daily tasks. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, with a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 65 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 30-year-old man comes to the clinic with a two-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and fatigue. He also reports soreness in his joints, particularly the knees and elbows. The patient notes that he has experienced significant weight loss over this period. On physical examination, he has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any regular medications. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, including an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A complete blood count shows mild anemia and leukocytosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive bilateral hip pain over the past six months. He states that the pain is worse in the morning and improves slightly throughout the day but never fully resolves. He has difficulty standing up from a seated position and experiences discomfort when climbing stairs. The patient denies any history of trauma or recent illnesses. His medical history is significant for obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in both hips with pain on internal rotation. Laboratory tests show slightly elevated ESR and CRP levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 60-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, weight loss, and joint pain over the past six months. He notes that his appetite has decreased significantly, and he has unintentionally lost about 15 pounds during this period. His past medical history is notable for hypertension, which is well controlled with lisinopril. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit 10 years ago. On examination, the patient appears pale, and there is mild swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.0 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 70 fL, and elevated inflammatory markers including an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. A peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells. Further investigations are planned. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive deformities of his fingers over the past few years. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves with activity. He also experiences occasional joint pain and swelling. His medical history is significant for a 20-year history of smoking, and he occasionally drinks alcohol. On examination, there is visible ulnar deviation of the fingers and swan-neck deformities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and bony erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He describes the stiffness as being most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving somewhat with activity. The pain is primarily located in his hands, wrists, and knees bilaterally. He also reports feeling fatigued and has experienced unintentional weight loss over the past few months. He has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain with some relief. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. A hand x-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man visits the clinic complaining of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past 6 months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. He denies any recent trauma or new physical activity. His family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of his hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 8 months. He notes the pain is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 75 mm/hr, and normal renal function tests. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions in the carpal bones. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's joint symptoms?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, primarily affecting his wrists and knees, and a new rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure. He reports feeling very tired and notes that the rash appears as a butterfly shape across his cheeks and nose. On further questioning, he admits to occasional chest pain and difficulty breathing, especially when taking deep breaths. Laboratory tests reveal anemia and elevated ANA titers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man visits the clinic due to difficulty walking and a tingling sensation in his right foot. He reports that the symptoms began several weeks ago and have progressively worsened. On further questioning, he admits to episodes of fatigue and some pain in his hands and knees. He denies any recent trauma or acute injuries. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and he is a nonsmoker. Neurologic examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the foot and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. There is no significant swelling in the joints. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department with left wrist pain after falling off his bicycle. The patient reports that he landed directly on his outstretched hand. He complains of swelling and inability to move the wrist without significant pain. On examination, there is tenderness over the anatomical snuffbox, swelling, and reduced range of motion of the left wrist. An x-ray is performed and shown to the attending physician. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Order an MRI of the wrist
options.B: Apply a wrist splint and arrange for outpatient follow-up
options.C: Immobilize the wrist with a thumb spica cast

question: A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic because of joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past year. He describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in his wrists and fingers. He has a history of smoking and reports occasional alcohol use. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling, and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. His laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray shows joint space narrowing and erosions in the hands. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He notes the swelling has been present for the past 3 months and primarily affects his wrists and fingers symmetrically. He denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in his metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of stiffness and pain in his hands, wrists, and knees. He reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for about two hours before improving. He also notes some swelling in these joints. His past medical history is significant only for seasonal allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for about two hours before improving. He reports fatigue and occasional fevers but denies any recent infections. On examination, there is swelling, tenderness, and limited range of motion in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male comes to the clinic complaining of skin tightening, especially on his fingers, and episodes where his fingers turn white and blue in the cold. He reports these symptoms started about a year ago and have progressively worsened. He also notes occasional joint pain, fatigue, and difficulty swallowing over the past few months. He denies any significant past medical history and has no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is noticeable sclerodactyly and digital pitting. Laboratory tests show positive ANA and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain and stiffness, particularly noticeable in the morning and lasting for more than an hour. He finds it difficult to get out of bed, but the pain improves with physical activity. His symptoms have been gradually worsening, and he has also developed pain in the buttocks which alternates between the left and right sides. He denies any history of trauma. The patient reports occasional fatigue but no weight loss or fevers. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. His father has a history of psoriasis. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male is admitted to the hospital with a two-week history of fever, night sweats, and fatigue. He reports intermittent headaches and a loss of appetite. His medical history is significant for hypertension and mild asthma. He denies any recent travel or contact with sick individuals. On physical examination, he has a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F) and a soft systolic murmur best heard at the left sternal border. Laboratory results reveal a white blood cell count of 12,000/mm³, elevated ESR, and C-reactive protein. Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 63-year-old male presents to the emergency department with swelling and severe pain in his right wrist after slipping on an icy sidewalk and falling on an outstretched hand. He reports immediate pain and an inability to move the wrist. He denies any numbness or tingling in the fingers. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On examination, there is swelling, bruising, and tenderness over the distal radius. The wrist is deformed, and there is a limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray is performed, revealing a fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management step for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes swelling in his fingers. On examination, the patient has symmetric synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. There are subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Considering the most likely diagnosis, which of the following is the best initial treatment?

A 68-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue for the past 3 months. He describes the pain as deep and aching, primarily affecting his lower back and pelvis. He denies any recent trauma or increased physical activity. His past medical history includes hypertension and a kidney stone episode last year. His family history reveals that his mother had osteoporosis and his father had a thyroid condition. On examination, the patient appears tired and has mild tenderness over the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum calcium levels and a low parathyroid hormone level. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the past few months due to weakness in his lower extremities. He reports frequent falls and difficulty climbing stairs. He also notes some pain and swelling in his knees and ankles, as well as fatigue and mild morning stiffness that resolves after an hour. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, there is bilateral weakness of the hip flexors and knee extensors. His reflexes are normal. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase (CK) levels and a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man visits the clinic due to a three-month history of joint pain and swelling in both his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty completing tasks at work. His medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical exam reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing is positive for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. A hand x-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He describes the pain as symmetric and affecting his wrists, fingers, and knees. The symptoms have been present for the past 6 months and are accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than one hour. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 135/80 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Hand X-rays reveal marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the past year. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. He notes some swelling and difficulty with gripping objects. On examination, there are multiple tender and swollen joints in the hands, including the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. There are palpable subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of chronic lower back pain that has been gradually worsening over the past few months. He describes the pain as aching and stiff, particularly in the mornings, which improves with physical activity. He reports some fatigue but denies any specific joint swelling. He has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. Physical examination reveals decreased spinal flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about 90 minutes before gradually improving throughout the day. The pain and stiffness predominantly affect the small joints of his hands and wrists symmetrically. He denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are tender and swollen. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) levels. His erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden, severe pain and swelling in his right big toe that started overnight. He reports experiencing similar episodes in the past, affecting different joints, but this is the most intense. His medical history is significant for obesity and occasional binge drinking. He denies fever or recent illness. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, swollen, and extremely tender to touch. Synovial fluid analysis reveals needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. He has multiple subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and ears. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling in his right arm. He reports that the neck pain has been present for several years, but the tingling sensation in his fingers started about two months ago and has gradually worsened. He denies any trauma or recent infections. The patient has a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is limited range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation in the right C6 dermatome. Reflexes are diminished in the right biceps. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain and stiffness. The pain is described as a deep ache that worsens with overhead activities. He is an office worker and experiences difficulty lifting objects above shoulder height. No history of trauma is reported. On examination, there is tenderness over the right shoulder anteriorly, and the range of motion is limited, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema. His past medical history includes asthma and obesity. Laboratory tests, including inflammatory markers, are within normal limits, and rheumatoid factor is negative. An MRI of the shoulder reveals thickening of the joint capsule and decreased joint space without significant synovitis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past 4 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty forming a fist. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past year. He describes morning stiffness lasting over two hours and difficulty performing daily tasks. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a high rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing with marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting his wrists and fingers. He reports the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about two hours before easing with movement. He denies any skin rashes or recent infections. His medical history is notable for treated hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, but no visible deformities. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized fatigue. He also reports experiencing night sweats and has noticed several areas of swelling around his neck and under his arms. He denies any weight loss, cough, or recent travel. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, multiple palpable, non-tender lymph nodes are noted in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory findings reveal a leukocyte count of 7,800/mm3 with 20% eosinophils and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A chest X-ray is performed and shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been worsening over the past year. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with activity throughout the day. His past medical history is notable for smoking 1 pack of cigarettes per day for 20 years. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the presence of subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue, joint pain predominantly in his knees and wrists, and recurrent nosebleeds. He reports bruising easily and feeling more tired than usual despite adequate sleep. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, he appears pale and has petechiae on his lower extremities. Joint examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness over the wrists and knees without redness or warmth. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,500/mm3, and platelet count of 80,000/mm3. Peripheral blood smear shows pancytopenia with no abnormal cells. His bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity with fat infiltration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of a lace-like purplish discoloration on his legs and a history of recurrent deep vein thromboses. He reports that the discoloration becomes more pronounced with cold exposure. He also mentions episodes of joint pain and morning stiffness which improve throughout the day. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities but no significant joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7500/mm3
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
Antinuclear antibody (ANA) positive
Rheumatoid factor negative
Antiphospholipid antibodies positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with increasing difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects over his head over the past six months. He reports feeling fatigued and notices that his thigh muscles appear to be shrinking. He has a history of hyperlipidemia and mild osteoarthritis in his knees. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness in the shoulders and hips, but no joint swelling or tenderness. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels and a slight elevation in inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of a reticular, net-like rash on his legs and recurrent episodes of leg swelling and pain. He reports having had two deep vein thromboses over the past year. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. On examination, you observe livedo reticularis on his lower extremities. Laboratory tests show a prolonged partial thromboplastin time (PTT), normal prothrombin time (PT), and a positive test for anti-cardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition causing this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old man with a 10-year history of joint pain visits his physician due to worsening symptoms in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with several subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show elevated levels of C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past several months. He also reports swelling near his jawline that fluctuates in size and mild joint pain in his fingers that worsens at the end of the day. He denies any significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement and a positive Schirmer test. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in his right big toe over the past year. Each episode lasts for about a week and resolves spontaneously. The patient reports that the attacks are precipitated by alcohol consumption. On physical examination, his right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, swollen, and tender to touch. Small, firm nodules are observed over his ear helix. Laboratory tests show elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling that developed after playing a game of soccer. He describes the pain as sharp and notes a significant increase in swelling overnight. He denies any previous history of joint issues but mentions occasional knee discomfort after exercise. He is in good general health and takes no medications. His family history is non-contributory. Physical examination shows a swollen, warm, and tender right knee with limited range of motion due to pain. Aspiration of the knee joint reveals cloudy fluid with numerous white blood cells but no crystals. Laboratory tests are notable for an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a rash and blood in his urine over the past week. The parents report that the rash appeared first on his lower legs and has been spreading upwards. They describe the rash as purplish spots that do not blanch when pressed. Additionally, they have noted that their son has been complaining of joint pain, particularly in his knees and ankles. The boy has no significant past medical history. On examination, there are palpable purpura on the lower extremities, mild swelling of the knees and ankles, and tenderness to palpation. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother because he has been complaining of joint pain that started two weeks ago. The pain initially began in his knees and ankles but has since moved to his elbows and wrists. He also experienced a sore throat three weeks ago, which resolved spontaneously. His mother notes that he has been more fatigued than usual. On examination, the patient has a temperature of 38.2°C (100.8°F). His heart rate is 90/min, respiratory rate is 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. On auscultation, a soft, blowing systolic murmur is heard at the apex of the heart. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while playing basketball. He reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist area. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. He denies any numbness or tingling in the fingers. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling over the distal radius, and the wrist is held in slight extension. There is no evidence of deformity or open wounds. An x-ray of the wrist shows a fracture of the distal radius with minimal displacement. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Application of heat to the area

question: A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of several small, painless lumps on his elbows and forearms that have developed over the past few months. He reports feeling tired and experiencing intermittent fevers and unintentional weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent travel, new medications, or dietary changes. Physical examination reveals multiple firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care provider with a 3-month history of progressive proximal muscle weakness. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and combing his hair. He also mentions mild joint pain, fatigue, and a 10-pound weight loss over the past two months. Physical examination reveals symmetrical weakness in the shoulder and hip girdle muscles, but no muscle tenderness. Laboratory tests show elevated muscle enzymes, including creatine kinase. EMG reveals myopathic changes, and a muscle biopsy shows endomysial inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 39-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue, joint pain, and a recent increase in blood pressure. He reports that over the past two weeks, his blood pressure has been consistently high at home, around 145/95 mmHg, despite having normal readings previously. He also mentions occasional swelling in his ankles. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any regular medications. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 148/92 mmHg, and there is mild tenderness around the small joints of his hands. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL and blood urea nitrogen of 45 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows moderate proteinuria without hematuria. Antibody tests reveal negative ANA and anti-dsDNA. ESR is elevated. Considering these findings, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of prolonged fatigue and joint pain affecting both wrists and knees for the past several months. He denies any recent trauma or infections. His family history is notable for his mother who had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, the patient has swelling and tenderness over the affected joints with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent episodes of skin discoloration and leg pain. He describes a net-like, bluish pattern on his legs, which becomes more prominent in cold weather. Over the past year, he has experienced several episodes of sharp pain in his calves, and he reports having been diagnosed with deep vein thrombosis twice. He denies any recent trauma or prolonged immobility. He is a non-smoker and works as an accountant. His family history is significant for a father who had early-onset stroke. On examination, his vital signs are within normal limits. His legs exhibit a reticular pattern of reddish-blue discoloration. Laboratory studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers but are otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 14-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his mother due to a rash on his legs and blood in his urine. The rash appeared a few days ago and is described as red-purple spots primarily located on his lower extremities. The patient has also been experiencing abdominal pain and joint discomfort in his knees and ankles over the last week. He denies any recent illness, changes in diet, or weight loss. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, palpable purpura is noted on the legs, and the abdomen is tender to palpation with no guarding or rebound. Laboratory tests show hematuria and mild proteinuria. Serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in both hands and knees for the past six months. He reports stiffness in the joints lasting more than an hour each morning, accompanied by swelling and warmth in the affected joints. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with lisinopril. He denies any recent infections or injuries. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor (RF) is positive, and anti-CCP antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of numbness and tingling in his hands and feet, which he has been experiencing for the past six months. He describes the sensations as a 'pins and needles' feeling that occasionally turns into a burning pain. He also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. His medical history is significant for well-controlled hypothyroidism, and he takes levothyroxine regularly. On physical examination, the patient has decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in a stocking-glove distribution. Reflexes are 1+ in the lower extremities and 2+ in the upper extremities. Laboratory studies show normal blood glucose levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive swelling and pain in his hands and wrists over the past 6 months. He notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. He denies history of trauma or recent infections. His medical history includes hypertension and obesity. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about 1.5 hours before it gradually improves with activity. He notes that the pain and swelling are primarily affecting his wrists and the small joints of his hands symmetrically. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows soft tissue swelling and juxta-articular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. He reports swelling in his wrists and finger joints, and notes that he has developed firm nodules under the skin near his elbows. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with subcutaneous nodules palpated over the extensor surface of the forearms. Laboratory studies show positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that the stiffness is most prominent in the morning and lasts for over an hour before gradually improving. He describes the pain as affecting both his hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in both hands and wrists that has persisted for over a year. He reports morning stiffness lasting several hours and difficulty using his hands for daily tasks. On examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory results reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male comes to the urgent care clinic with complaints of acute right knee pain and swelling following a weekend soccer match. He describes a sudden, sharp pain during a tackle and has noticed swelling and difficulty bearing weight on the affected knee since. He denies any history of similar joint issues or significant medical conditions. His family history is non-contributory. Physical examination reveals an effusion in the right knee with limited range of motion due to pain. There is tenderness along the joint line, but no erythema or warmth. Anterior drawer test is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Knee aspiration and synovial fluid analysis
options.E: Application of a knee brace and rest

question: A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising. He mentions that the joint pain is mostly in his wrists and knees and is sometimes accompanied by low-grade fevers and night sweats. He has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears pale and has petechiae on his lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,000/mm³, and platelets of 75,000/mm³. A peripheral blood smear shows teardrop-shaped red blood cells and immature white blood cells. A bone marrow biopsy is planned. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He has noticed swelling in his wrists and small joints of his hands, accompanied by firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-managed. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of swelling and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past four months. He mentions that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about two hours. He also reports feeling fatigued and having difficulty performing daily tasks. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in his hands and wrists, which worsens in the morning and gradually improves over the course of several hours. He also reports feeling fatigue and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory testing reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as the presence of rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with joint pain and skin lesions. He reports a several-month history of swollen and painful joints, particularly affecting the fingers of his right hand and left knee. He also mentions patches of thick, red skin with silvery scales on his elbows and scalp that have been present for the last year. The patient denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. Blood tests reveal elevated acute phase reactants, but rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of chronic nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and fatigue over the past six months. He also reports several episodes of hemoptysis and unintentional weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any known allergies. On examination, his blood pressure is 148/92 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and his temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C). Auscultation of the lungs reveals scattered crackles. Laboratory tests show elevated creatinine levels and a positive C-ANCA. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. The urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, improving with activity. He denies recent trauma or fever. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. On examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr and a positive rheumatoid factor. An x-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old male presents to the clinic complaining of diffuse body aches and fatigue that began a week ago after recovering from a viral upper respiratory illness. He reports persistent muscle soreness and joint pain that worsens with movement. He denies any swelling, redness, or warmth around the joints. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, he appears tired but is in no acute distress. His vital signs are normal, and there is no evidence of joint deformities or skin rashes. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, normal renal function, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and tender red nodules on his shins. He reports that the joint pain started about two weeks ago and is mostly affecting his knees and ankles. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or chills but mentions fatigue. He works as a school teacher and denies recent travel or new medications. Review of systems is significant for mild weight loss over the past month. His temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there are several erythematous, tender nodules on both shins and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Chest X-ray is ordered and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in his wrists and fingers for the past four months. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. He has noticed similar symptoms in his knees. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, well controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are present. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers that have been occurring intermittently for the past year. He also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in the knees and ankles. On examination, there are shallow ulcerations on the oral mucosa and erythematous nodules on the scrotum. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers but negative for rheumatoid factor. His medical history is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 78-year-old man presents to the clinic with severe back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy object. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region, without any radiation. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or neurological symptoms. His medical history is notable for a 5-year history of chronic back pain, managed with occasional over-the-counter analgesics. He is a lifelong nonsmoker and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T8 vertebra and a mild kyphotic posture. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. A lateral spine X-ray reveals a wedge-shaped compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He reports experiencing morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in multiple joints, including his wrists and knees, for the past several months. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is currently well-managed with medication. He denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 70 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of numbness and tingling in his feet that began several months ago and has gradually worsened. He also reports a burning sensation in his toes and occasional sharp pains. He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin. He denies any recent trauma or changes in medications. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to pinprick and vibration in a stocking-glove pattern. The patient's reflexes are diminished at the ankles and knees. His hemoglobin A1c is 6.5%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 40-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain that has been progressively worsening over the past few months. He reports feeling extremely tired even after a full night's sleep and experiences joint pain in his wrists and knees, which is more pronounced in the mornings but improves slightly as the day progresses. He denies any significant medical history. On physical examination, there is pallor and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia, with a hemoglobin level of 9.0 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 3,200/mm3, and a platelet count of 110,000/mm3. Reticulocyte count is low, and serum vitamin B12 and folate levels are normal. The rheumatoid factor is negative. A bone marrow biopsy is performed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that developed three weeks after a hiking trip in a wooded area. He describes the joint pain as migratory, affecting his knees and ankles predominantly. He also noticed a red rash on his legs and trunk, which he describes as having a target-like appearance. He reports feeling fatigued and having low-grade fevers intermittently. He has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, there is a maculopapular rash with some target lesions on his trunk, and mild swelling of the knees is noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A serological test for Borrelia burgdorferi is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent lower back pain that has been worsening over the past year. The pain is particularly severe in the mornings and tends to improve with physical activity as the day progresses. He reports feeling stiff for about an hour after waking up. He denies any significant trauma to the area, and there is no history of fever, weight loss, or bowel disturbances. His medical history is unremarkable except for episodic anterior uveitis. Examination reveals limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressive joint deformity and stiffness in his hands and wrists. The symptoms are worse in the morning and improve with activity throughout the day. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, which is controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and deformity of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular erosions and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to his lower back. He has a history of osteoporosis treated with calcium and vitamin D supplements. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lumbar vertebrae and restricted range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal. A lateral x-ray of the spine shows a wedge-shaped deformity of the L3 vertebra. Laboratory studies show normal calcium, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the hands and wrists, that has persisted for the past few months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour before improving. He has also noticed swelling in the joints of his fingers and difficulty performing tasks that require fine motor skills. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of a rash on his face and arms that worsens with sun exposure. He also reports joint pain in his wrists and knees, and has noticed occasional oral ulcers. He denies fever, weight loss, or recent travel history. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. His family history is unremarkable. On examination, there is an erythematous rash across his cheeks and the bridge of his nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. There are small, painless oral ulcers present. His joints are tender but without swelling. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive joint pain and swelling over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and has noticed deformities developing in multiple joints of the hands. His medical history is notable for hypertension, treated with lisinopril, and hyperlipidemia. On examination, he has swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with visible ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over the past several months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving slightly with activity. The pain primarily affects his wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints symmetrically. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, his joints are swollen and tender, and there is a decreased range of motion in affected areas. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). His rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) is high. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a persistent rash on his face and joint pain. He notes that the rash becomes more pronounced after spending time outdoors. The patient also reports occasional oral ulcers and feeling more fatigued than usual. He denies fever or weight changes. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose that spares the nasolabial folds. There is tenderness and slight swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a mild anemia and elevated antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent lymphadenopathy and night sweats. He has also experienced unintentional weight loss and intermittent fever. He denies smoking, alcohol use, or any recent infections. On physical examination, there are palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are non-tender and mobile. There is no noticeable splenomegaly or hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests show elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and mild anemia. An excisional biopsy of one of the lymph nodes is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old male presents to the hospital with a persistent fever lasting for three weeks. He reports experiencing generalized fatigue, night sweats, and a 5 kg weight loss over the past month. The patient denies any significant past medical or family history. Physical examination reveals mild hepatosplenomegaly and non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, elevated C-reactive protein (CRP), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), as well as normal white blood cell and platelet counts. Blood cultures are negative. A chest radiograph is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.C: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

question: A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive weakness in his shoulders and hips over the past few months. He notes difficulty climbing stairs and lifting heavy objects. Additionally, he mentions mild joint pain and fatigue. He denies any significant past medical history. Physical examination reveals 3/5 strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities and 5/5 strength in the distal muscle groups. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to joint pain and swelling. Over the past week, he has experienced pain that migrates from his elbows to his knees and ankles. He also had a sore throat about two weeks ago, which resolved without medical treatment. He denies fever, but his mother mentions he's been more fatigued than usual. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. Examination reveals swollen, tender joints with a limited range of motion. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 112/72 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer is also elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Refer to rheumatology for juvenile idiopathic arthritis
options.E: Start nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for symptomatic relief
options.F: Monitor symptoms and schedule a follow-up in one month

question: A 58-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive swelling and deformity of his fingers over the past 6 months. He notes that his fingers are stiff in the morning for about an hour. He reports intermittent mild fever and fatigue but denies any recent infections or other systemic symptoms. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints, and tenderness upon palpation of the wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and erosions at the wrist and finger joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and states that his mother had similar symptoms. He denies any recent travel or infections. His temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical exam reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic because of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He reports that he had a mild viral upper respiratory infection shortly before these symptoms began. He experiences generalized joint pain that is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day, but it does not last as long as an hour. He denies any swelling or redness in the joints. He has no significant medical history and does not take any medications. Physical examination shows no joint deformities or swelling. Laboratory studies reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He describes morning stiffness that typically lasts for more than an hour. He has also noticed some fatigue and weight loss. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. He reports feeling more tired than usual and has trouble concentrating. He also mentions having occasional muscle aches but denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-managed with lisinopril. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, his skin appears dry and cool, and he has a slight swelling in the neck. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated TSH level with low free T4. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He describes the pain as intermittent and notes that it primarily affects his fingers and right knee. He also reports scaly, erythematous skin lesions on his elbows and knees, which have been present for several years. The patient denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes and has no history of fevers, weight loss, or oral ulcers. His medical history is significant only for mild hypertension. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the right knee, and several distal interphalangeal joints are enlarged and tender. The skin lesions are consistent with psoriasis. Laboratory tests reveal negative rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He notes that over the past few months, he has had stiffness in his fingers and wrists, particularly in the morning, which improves throughout the day. He also mentions difficulty with daily tasks due to pain in both knees and elbows. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of pain and swelling in his wrists and fingers, which is worst in the morning and improves throughout the day. He reports difficulty making a fist in the morning. He denies any trauma, but does mention a family history of 'arthritis problems.' Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Hand radiographs demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of joint pains in his hands and wrists for the last six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache associated with morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, improving as the day progresses. His medical history is notable for smoking one pack per day for 20 years, which he quit two years ago. Family history reveals his mother had osteoarthritis. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care doctor with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past six months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes swelling in his joints. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and elevated C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness primarily affecting his right knee and left wrist. He also notes the presence of scaly, red patches on his elbows and knees that have been persistent for the past year. He reports occasional fatigue but no significant weight loss or fever. On physical examination, there is mild swelling of the affected joints and clear psoriatic plaques over the extensor surfaces. He denies any family history of arthritis. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to his healthcare provider with a 6-month history of joint pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in his hands and wrists. He has difficulty performing daily tasks due to pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning. His medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is increased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man visits his physician due to persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. He describes the pain as symmetrical and affecting both his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. His medical history is unremarkable, except for occasional episodes of mild depression. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests are significant for a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 40 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty using his hands due to joint pain. His past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show:
C-reactive protein: 25 mg/L
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 48 mm/hr
Serum rheumatoid factor: Positive
