Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: Positive
Radiographs of the hands demonstrate soft tissue swelling and marginal erosions at the joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic with persistent bone pain, particularly in his lower back, over the past three months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that intensifies at night and is not relieved by standard NSAIDs. He denies any history of trauma. He reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has noticed increased thirst and urination. His medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes a day. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the lower lumbar spine but is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
Platelet count 190,000/mm3
Serum
Calcium 11.9 mg/dL
Phosphate 3.2 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 92 U/L
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) 90 pg/mL (N = 10-65)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man is evaluated in the emergency department for a painful, purplish skin rash on his thighs that he first noticed 2 days ago. He reports a history of multiple episodes of leg swelling and calf tenderness over the past year, which were diagnosed as deep vein thromboses. He is currently on warfarin therapy. In addition to the rash, he describes a new onset of joint pain and occasional headaches. His medical history is significant for migraines and treated hypertension. Physical examination shows livedo reticularis on the thighs and tenderness in the lower extremity joints without significant swelling. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated inflammatory markers, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, headache, and swelling in his legs over the past month. He notes that his blood pressure has been consistently elevated at home, reading 150/95 mmHg on average, despite no previous history of hypertension. He reports bilateral knee pain, which he attributes to aging, and has morning stiffness lasting about 15 minutes. Physical exam reveals 2+ pitting edema in both lower extremities and mild hypertensive retinopathy. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatinine at 1.8 mg/dL and a BUN of 24 mg/dL. Urinalysis is positive for proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.C: Chronic kidney disease secondary to hypertension

question: A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening over his fingers and color changes in his fingers when exposed to cold for the past several months. He describes episodes where his fingers turn white then blue and finally red upon warming. He also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. He denies any significant past medical history and has not seen a doctor in years. On physical examination, there is evidence of taut skin over the fingers and some mild swelling of the joints. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of headaches and fatigue over the past month. He has also noticed swelling in his legs and occasional joint pain. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. On examination, his blood pressure is 170/100 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine and urea levels, and urinalysis shows proteinuria and hematuria. An ultrasound of the kidneys reveals bilateral small, echogenic kidneys. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 52-year-old male presents to your clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the last few months. He reports feeling cold more often than usual and has noticed some swelling in his neck. On examination, you observe a slightly enlarged thyroid gland. Laboratory tests reveal elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. He also mentions occasional joint pain, primarily in his hands and knees, which has been attributed to his longstanding history of gout. What is the most likely diagnosis for his current condition?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic due to multiple subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and knees that have developed over the past few months. He also reports intermittent fever and a 10-pound weight loss over the last three months. On further questioning, he mentions occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals several firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP. A biopsy of one of the nodules reveals necrotizing granulomatous inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old man presents to the clinic with severe mid-back pain that began suddenly after he lifted a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the middle of his back, without any radiation. He denies any history of trauma or recent falls. His medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension and osteoporosis, for which he takes alendronate. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the thoracic vertebrae, but no spinal deformity or neurological deficits are noted. Laboratory investigations reveal normal calcium and vitamin D levels, with a slight elevation in alkaline phosphatase. A thoracic spine X-ray shows a compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Vertebral compression fracture due to osteoporosis

question: A 48-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of unexplained weight gain and puffiness around his eyes. He reports that his shoes and rings are tighter than usual. Additionally, he notes morning joint stiffness lasting about an hour, particularly in the knees and elbows. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and diffuse joint tenderness without synovitis. Laboratory studies show normal thyroid function tests, a negative rheumatoid factor, and proteinuria on urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the last 3 months. He also reports feeling unusually cold and has noticed pallor of his skin. He denies any significant joint pain, but mentions occasional stiffness in his hands upon waking. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is well-controlled with metformin. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 70 fL, and low serum ferritin levels. Further examination reveals no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.G: Test for occult blood in stool

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. He reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning, lasting approximately two hours before improving as the day progresses. He notices swelling in his wrists and fingers, making it difficult to perform daily tasks. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory findings show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes pain and swelling in his wrists, knees, and ankles. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He works as an accountant and does not smoke or drink alcohol.

On physical examination, the patient appears fatigued. His wrists and knees are tender and swollen with limited range of motion. Laboratory findings reveal hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL with a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 75 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 25 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is positive at a high titer, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are present.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic due to increasing difficulty climbing stairs and getting up from chairs over the past three months. He also reports muscle pain and fatigue but denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. On examination, he has proximal muscle weakness in both the upper and lower limbs. He has no sensory deficits. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Electromyography reveals myopathic changes. Further laboratory testing shows a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with joint pain in both of his wrists and hands that has been progressively worsening over the last 6 months. The pain is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about two hours and improves as the day progresses. His medical history is unremarkable except for a family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, the patient has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, stiffness, and swelling, particularly in his wrists and fingers. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. His medical history is notable for mild hypertension, well-managed with diet and exercise. He denies any recent infections or trauma. On examination, there are tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, but no erythema. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are significantly elevated. Hand x-rays show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of progressive weakness in his shoulders and hips over the past 3 months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head, but denies any significant joint pain. He has a past medical history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He denies tobacco use and drinks alcohol rarely. On physical examination, he has 4/5 strength in his proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities, with normal strength distally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase (CK) levels and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and notes difficulty in performing daily tasks due to the pain. He has a history of cardiovascular disease managed with atorvastatin and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recurring episodes of blotchy, purplish skin discoloration primarily on his legs for the past six months. He also reports having had two episodes of deep vein thrombosis in the last year, treated with anticoagulation therapy. He denies any joint pain, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, livedo reticularis is noted on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and renal function. The patient has a negative rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA). However, testing reveals the presence of lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother because of a 3-week history of joint pain. The pain has been affecting multiple joints, particularly the knees and wrists, and tends to migrate from one joint to another. The mother reports that he had a sore throat about a month ago, which resolved without treatment. He has been feeling tired, and his school performance has declined recently. On physical examination, the patient appears well-nourished but slightly pale. His temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F). The joints are tender to palpation, but there is no swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain, facial puffiness, particularly around the eyes, and stiffness in his joints, especially in the morning. He notes that over the last six months, his rings have become tight, and he has difficulty wearing shoes that fit comfortably. He also mentions feeling fatigued and having a reduced appetite. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and non-pitting edema in the hands and feet. Laboratory tests show normal creatinine levels, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and proteinuria on urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. He has also noticed swollen lymph nodes in his neck and armpits. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He denies recent infections, travel, or use of intravenous drugs. On examination, there are multiple firm, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory studies reveal mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in his wrists and fingers. He notes that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for about 1.5 hours before improving throughout the day. The patient denies any recent trauma or infection. He has a background of hypertension, controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory workup reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 56-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with recurrent episodes of severe right big toe pain, swelling, and redness over the past year. Each episode lasts for several days and seems to be triggered by dietary indulgences. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he is taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint. There are palpable nodules over the helix of the ears. Laboratory tests during the episodes show elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetric pain in his hands and knees. He has no significant past medical history but reports his mother had similar joint issues. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, hematocrit of 33%, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports that the pain is particularly severe in the mornings and affects his wrists, knees, and small joints of his hands symmetrically. He denies any recent trauma or new medications. He has a history of smoking and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals warmth and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show:
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 12 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody positive
X-rays of the hands show narrowing of joint spaces and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports that the pain is particularly in his wrists and knees, and he experiences stiffness in the morning lasting about an hour. He also notes recent unintentional weight loss and occasional low-grade fevers. He denies any recent infections or trauma. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of dry eyes and a dry mouth for the past year. He notes that he frequently needs to sip water to eat dry foods and often wakes up at night feeling that his mouth is too dry. Additionally, he reports bilateral swelling in his cheeks, which he believes are his 'glands,' and occasional mild joint pain. He denies any significant systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. His medical history is notable for hypertension treated with amlodipine. On examination, there is enlargement of the parotid glands bilaterally and reduced tear production is noted upon testing with a Schirmer's test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of persistent pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. His medical history reveals a previous diagnosis of hypertension, which is well-controlled on medication. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 1-year history of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and can last several hours before improving. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate symmetric joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-year history of joint pain and progressive deformity in his hands. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in multiple joints, including his wrists and the proximal interphalangeal joints of his fingers. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is ulnar deviation at the metacarpophalangeal joints and swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment to slow disease progression in this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of persistent nasal congestion, sinus pain, and occasional episodes of nasal bleeding. He also reports a chronic cough productive of yellowish sputum and increasing fatigue over the past few weeks. He has experienced joint pain and swelling, primarily in the small joints of his hands. His past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and mild edema in the hands. Laboratory tests show an elevated creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL and a urine analysis reveals hematuria and proteinuria. A chest x-ray shows bilateral patchy pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past 3 months, and generalized weakness. He reports feeling unusually cold and having a decreased appetite. Upon reviewing his medical history, he has a remote history of peptic ulcer disease. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 70 fL, and low ferritin levels. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 24-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain that has been migrating from one joint to another over the past two weeks. He describes the pain as primarily affecting his knees, wrists, and elbows. He recalls having a sore throat about 3 weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. He denies fever, skin rashes, or weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, there is mild swelling and tenderness in his left knee and right wrist. There is no erythema or warmth over the joints. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rapid antigen detection test for Group A Streptococcus is negative, but a throat culture has not been performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man visits his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and symmetric swelling in his hands and wrists. He denies any history of trauma or recent infections. His medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory evaluation reveals a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, a normal mean corpuscular volume, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with fatigue and joint pain. He reports a 6-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in the joints of his hands, wrists, and knees. His medical history includes hypertension and he is a former smoker. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the last 8 weeks. He notes morning stiffness lasting for more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. The patient has no significant past medical history but mentions that his mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Laboratory work-up reveals an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serum rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. An X-ray of the hands shows marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling, predominantly affecting his hands and wrists. He also reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, and he is a non-smoker. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacological treatment for this patient?

A 50-year-old man comes to the clinic due to increasing bilateral hip pain over the past six months. He describes the pain as dull and aching, which worsens with prolonged activity, especially after his daily walks. He denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. He has no significant past medical history and takes over-the-counter pain medication occasionally. He reports no recent trauma or significant weight changes. Physical examination reveals limited internal rotation of both hips and tenderness on palpation over the lateral aspects. His body mass index is 28 kg/m². Laboratory studies show:
C-reactive protein 2.1 mg/L
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 20 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor negative
Antinuclear antibody negative
A pelvic x-ray shows joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis of both hip joints without erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue. He reports that these symptoms started about a week ago after recovering from a severe viral illness characterized by fever and sore throat. He denies any joint swelling, redness, or significant morning stiffness but mentions a general feeling of tiredness and mild myalgia. He has no significant medical history and is not taking any medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, normal liver and kidney function tests, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly in the small joints of his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and progressively worsening joint deformities. His medical history is significant for a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation, swan-neck deformities, and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with concerns about progressive deformities in his fingers over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and significant swelling in his hands and wrists. His medical history reveals occasional joint pain over the last few years. Physical examination shows ulnar deviation and swan neck deformities of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with these findings?

A 45-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. He describes the pain as dull and achy, and it is exacerbated with overhead activities or lifting objects. Over the past few weeks, he has also noticed a progressive decrease in the range of motion of his shoulder, particularly impacting his ability to reach for items above his head or behind his back. He denies any recent trauma but notes a history of playing recreational basketball. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder, and passive range of motion is reduced compared to the left shoulder. Strength testing reveals mild weakness in the shoulder abductor muscles. An x-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. He reports swelling in his neck that he first noticed approximately 2 months ago. On examination, multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes are palpated in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. A lymph node biopsy is performed, and histopathology demonstrates a mixed cellular infiltrate with Reed-Sternberg cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and an unintended weight loss of 10 pounds over the past two months. He also notes occasional joint pain in his hands and knees but attributes it to 'getting older.' His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent changes in diet or exercise habits. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild hepatomegaly. Initial laboratory workup shows normocytic anemia with a low reticulocyte count. Serum ferritin is elevated, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is increased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. On examination, there is synovitis in the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with a two-year history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and occasional genital ulcers. He also reports joint pain in his knees and ankles, along with fatigue that has worsened over the past several months. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, there are several shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and a few healing lesions on the scrotum. No synovitis is observed in the joints, and vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man visits the clinic due to progressive deformity and stiffness in his fingers and wrists over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty using his hands for daily tasks. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is swelling and erythema over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents for evaluation of persistent fevers and generalized fatigue that have been ongoing for three weeks. He reports an unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds during this period. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. He denies any recent travel or new medications. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness and swelling in his wrists and knees, with no apparent synovitis. Initial laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Complete blood count is notable for a normocytic anemia. Blood cultures are obtained and are negative. A chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.G: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

question: A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a mottled purplish discoloration on his legs that has worsened over the past few months. He reports several episodes of pain and swelling in his calves that resolved spontaneously. He has a history of hypertension and smokes one pack of cigarettes per day. His medications include lisinopril and aspirin. On examination, livedo reticularis is noted on both lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and creatinine levels, with elevated D-dimer. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is negative, but his antiphospholipid antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with chronic pain and stiffness in his wrists and hands that has been progressively worsening over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as several subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Hand radiographs show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, significant fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep that have been persistent over the past several months. He reports difficulty focusing and a sense of mental fog. He denies any recent trauma, weight changes, or fever. His past medical history includes mild intermittent asthma managed with an inhaler. Physical examination reveals tender points in the trapezius muscles, lateral epicondyles, and knees. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and chemistries, and a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody tests are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic with a chief complaint of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that becomes sharper with activity and is relieved somewhat by rest. He denies any morning stiffness lasting longer than 15 minutes and states that his symptoms are generally worse by the end of the day. He has a history of mild hyperlipidemia treated with atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is tenderness upon palpation of the greater trochanteric region of both hips, and range of motion testing is limited by pain, particularly in abduction and internal rotation. The patient has a normal gait, and his vital signs are within normal limits. A pelvis X-ray shows joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis, particularly in the superolateral aspect of the femoral heads. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic due to chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain persisting for over a year. He describes morning stiffness lasting at least an hour and swelling in these areas. He reports feeling increasingly fatigued and has noticed small, firm lumps on his elbows. He has no significant past medical history except for occasional headaches. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints in both hands. The elbows have subcutaneous nodules. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands reveals erosions and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic because of joint pain and stiffness lasting for about two hours each morning for the past six months. He reports pain in his wrists, the proximal interphalangeal, and metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands. He has a past medical history significant for mild hypertension and is on lisinopril. He denies fever, recent infections, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender joints in a symmetric distribution involving the wrists and fingers. Laboratory studies show:
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate elevated
C-reactive protein elevated
An x-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to his primary care physician complaining of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain that has been present for several months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks that require fine motor skills. He notes the development of subcutaneous nodules over his elbows and fingers. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and a positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of his hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old man visits the clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. He reports weakness in his legs, which has made it difficult for him to climb stairs or walk for extended periods. He denies any recent trauma or falls. He has noted occasional tingling in his feet and a sense of imbalance. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. Neurological examination reveals decreased strength in the lower extremities, diminished reflexes at the knees and ankles, and decreased vibration sense in the feet. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to his physician complaining of gradually worsening weakness. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects over his head. He also mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing mild joint pain in his shoulders and hips. He denies any recent infections, travel, or changes in medications. On examination, he has weakness in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities but intact distal muscle strength. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with difficulty walking, which he noticed a few weeks ago. He reports that he often trips over his toes and has increasing weakness in his right leg. He denies any trauma, fever, or weight loss. The patient has a history of poorly controlled diabetes mellitus and is currently on insulin therapy. He has also noticed some numbness in the lower leg and foot. Physical examination reveals foot drop on the right side and decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the dorsum of the right foot. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ in the right knee and absent at the right ankle. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 47-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that developed after a hiking trip two weeks ago. He recalls removing a tick from his left thigh a few days after the trip. The rash initially appeared at the bite site and then expanded into a large, erythematous ring with central clearing. He also reports fatigue and muscle aches. On examination, there is a characteristic annular rash on the left thigh, consistent with an erythema migrans lesion. Laboratory tests reveal mildly elevated inflammatory markers but negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with persistent nasal congestion and recurrent sinus infections over the past six months. He denies any seasonal allergies or recent upper respiratory infections. Over the last few weeks, he has also noticed increasing fatigue and joint pain in his knees and wrists. He reports unintentional weight loss of approximately 7 kg (15 lbs) over the last two months. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 140/85 mm Hg, and pulse is 78/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and mild edema in the wrists and knees without erythema. Laboratory results show elevated serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL and a positive c-ANCA (cytoplasmic anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies). A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. He reports that the pain tends to improve as the day progresses. He also notes the presence of small, firm lumps under the skin near his elbows. His medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with medication. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting about two hours, which improves with activity. He denies any recent trauma or infections. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with wrist pain after falling off his bicycle. He landed on his outstretched hand. The pain is localized to the distal radius, and there is noticeable swelling and tenderness. An x-ray is performed, and a transverse fracture of the distal radius is observed. The patient reports a history of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about an hour for the past few months. He denies any significant past medical history, and his vital signs are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for his current presentation?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy box at home. He has a history of chronic glucocorticoid use for polymyalgia rheumatica. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. He denies any bowel or bladder incontinence. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits are noted. His vital signs are normal. A plain radiograph of the thoracic spine reveals a vertebral compression fracture at T8. He has been experiencing some stiffness in the mornings, particularly in his hands, but it resolves within an hour. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 23-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure, as well as joint pain primarily affecting his knees and wrists. He also reports intermittent fever and fatigue. On further questioning, he mentions episodes of painful oral ulcers over the past few months. He denies any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash across the bridge of his nose and cheeks, sparing the nasolabial folds, and mild swelling in his knee joints. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 12 g/dL, leukocyte count of 4,000/mm3, and a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of chronic lower back pain. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity as the day progresses. Over the past year, he has also noticed stiffness in his lower back, particularly in the early morning, that lasts for over an hour. He denies any significant joint pain in his hands or feet. He occasionally feels fatigue but denies any fevers or weight loss. On physical examination, there is limited flexion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. HLA-B27 antigen is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain and progressively restricted range of motion, particularly during overhead activities and reaching behind his back. He denies any recent trauma or overuse. He describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens at night, making it difficult to sleep on his right side. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, the right shoulder reveals limited abduction and external rotation with pain at the extremes of motion. There is no erythema or warmth over the joint. Strength testing of the rotator cuff muscles is normal, but there is discomfort with passive range of motion. The rest of the musculoskeletal and systemic examination is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the nephrology clinic with concerns of new-onset hypertension and mild fatigue over the last few months. He denies any recent weight change, skin rashes, or joint pain. His medical history is significant for hyperlipidemia, which is managed with a statin. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, his blood pressure is 170/105 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, with a previous baseline of 1.0 mg/dL six months ago, and mild proteinuria. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows asymmetrical kidney size with the right kidney measuring significantly smaller than the left. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic with widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping that have persisted for the past six months. He describes the pain as a constant, dull ache that affects his muscles and joints throughout the body. He notes that he feels tired even after a full night's sleep and has trouble concentrating at work. His medical history is significant for anxiety, but he denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points over the neck, shoulders, elbows, and knees, but no joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests are unremarkable, including normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with bilateral hand and wrist pain that has persisted for the past year. He describes the pain as achy and notes significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour, accompanied by swelling in his wrists and small joints of the hands. His medical history is notable for high blood pressure, controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent episodes of severe pain in his right big toe for the last 6 months. Each episode lasts about 2 to 3 days and resolves spontaneously. He reports swelling and redness during these episodes, which usually occur after consuming alcohol or a large meal. He has no significant past medical history. He does not smoke but drinks wine often during social events. Physical examination reveals a swollen, tender right big toe with a firm, nodular mass over the joint. Laboratory findings show elevated serum uric acid levels. Joint aspiration reveals needle-shaped crystals under polarized light. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that becomes more pronounced after physical activity. He reports morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes, which improves as the day goes on. He denies any joint swelling, fever, or significant weight loss. He has a family history of osteoarthritis and his past medical history includes hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips, but no visible swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Radiographs of the hips reveal joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent dry mouth and dry eyes over the past six months. He also reports bilateral swelling and discomfort in his parotid glands. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and he denies any history of autoimmune disorders. Physical examination reveals noticeable enlargement of both parotid glands and mild joint tenderness in his wrists and knees. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He reports generalized fatigue and occasional fever. His medical history includes controlled type 2 diabetes. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), and a positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment to reduce this patient's joint symptoms and inflammation?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations that radiate to his right arm. He reports that the symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past six months. He works as an office administrator and spends long hours at a desk. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion in the neck and decreased sensation to light touch in the right thumb and index finger. Deep tendon reflexes in the upper extremities are diminished. Laboratory studies show mild elevation of C-reactive protein but normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with chronic pain in both hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past 5 years. He reports that the pain is associated with stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. He has noticed swelling in his knuckles and the presence of firm, non-tender nodules near his elbows. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. The patient's family history reveals that his mother had similar joint problems. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation. Serological tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past few months, along with progressive periorbital edema and morning joint stiffness that improves throughout the day. He denies any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. He works a desk job and reports a well-balanced diet, with no recent changes in his activity level or caloric intake. On examination, he has noticeable swelling around the eyes and mild tenderness of the small joints in the hands without any obvious deformities. His vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory results show normal renal function, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of unexplained weight gain over the past 6 months, accompanied by periorbital edema and stiffness in his knees and wrists, especially in the morning. He reports that his clothes have become tighter, and his face appears puffier than usual. He denies any significant changes in diet or physical activity. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. On examination, his blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, and he has noticeable periorbital swelling. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood counts, but urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria with no hematuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive muscle weakness over the past several months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He denies joint pain, rash, or respiratory symptoms. His past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, he exhibits symmetric proximal muscle weakness in the shoulders and hips. His laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Electromyography reveals muscle irritability and myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man visits his family doctor due to a rash on his face that he has noticed gets worse after spending time in the sun. He also reports joint pain in his hands and knees, which he describes as particularly stiff in the mornings. He mentions feeling more fatigued than usual over the past few months. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any regular medications. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash over his cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is tenderness and slight swelling in the metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's presentation?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that started approximately two weeks ago after recovering from a viral upper respiratory tract infection. He reports feeling generally unwell and lethargic but denies any joint swelling or significant morning stiffness. He has no significant past medical history. On examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. His vital signs are stable, and there is no evidence of joint deformity or skin rashes. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He describes the pain as affecting both wrists and knees symmetrically, with stiffness lasting for over an hour in the mornings. He reports no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling over the metacarpophalangeal joints and knees bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin 11 g/dL, Hematocrit 33%, Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 90 fL, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 75 mm/hr, Rheumatoid factor positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain. He describes the pain as a constant dull ache that is worse in the mornings and lasts for several hours. He notes stiffness and swelling in his fingers and wrists, making it difficult to open jars or type on a keyboard. His medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals synovitis and subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), as well as positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 48-year-old male presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall while playing basketball. He landed on his outstretched hand. The pain is severe and he is unable to move his wrist or grip objects without discomfort. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the distal radius. An x-ray of the wrist reveals a fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation. The patient has a history of occasional joint pain in his hands and knees, and his father had rheumatoid arthritis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Application of a wrist brace and follow-up

question: A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour for the past six months. He reports the stiffness primarily affects his wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints symmetrically. He describes the pain as worse in the morning and gradually improving as the day progresses. On examination, synovitis is noted in the affected joints, and he has difficulty making a fist. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are detected. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and generalized joint pain for the past two weeks. He reports feeling fatigued and having a low-grade fever. His medical history is unremarkable, although he recalls having a sore throat about a month ago. On physical examination, tender erythematous nodules are noted on the anterior aspect of both legs. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Chest X-ray is ordered and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, which is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He has also noticed the development of small, firm nodules under his skin near his elbows. He denies any significant weight loss, fevers, or recent infections. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of his arms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with acute onset of joint pain, predominantly affecting his knees and ankles, for the past week. He reports recent travel to Southeast Asia for a business trip. The pain is associated with swelling and stiffness, particularly severe in the mornings. He denies any rash or skin lesions. He also mentions malaise and intermittent fever during the past few days. Physical examination reveals warm, swollen joints with restricted range of motion. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. His rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy box at work. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back region, with some radiation to the sides but no radiculopathy. He denies any history of trauma or falls. His medical history is significant for chronic glucocorticoid use due to polymyalgia rheumatica. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the thoracic vertebrae, and his range of motion is limited due to pain. Neurological examination is normal, with no motor or sensory deficits. An X-ray of the spine reveals a wedge-shaped vertebral compression fracture at T8. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.B: Pain management with acetaminophen and possible vertebroplasty
options.F: MRI of the spine to assess for spinal cord compression

question: A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic due to recurrent episodes of painful oral and genital ulcers over the past two years. He reports that these episodes occur every few months, with each episode lasting about two weeks. He also describes occasional joint pain in his knees and ankles, but denies swelling or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. He denies any recent travel, new medication usage, or changes in his diet. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and a few healing ulcers on the scrotum. There is mild tenderness over the knee joints, but no synovitis is noted. Complete blood count is normal, with a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, swelling, and stiffness. The stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, often lasting over one hour. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, along with the presence of firm subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions at the metacarpophalangeal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with multiple subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and knees that have developed over the last few months. He reports joint pain, fatigue, and intermittent low-grade fevers. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He denies any recent infections, and his family history is unremarkable. On examination, the nodules are firm and non-tender. Laboratory testing reveals a mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in his right big toe over the past year. He reports the episodes typically occur after consuming red meat and alcohol, lasting for a few days and resolving spontaneously. He denies any significant morning stiffness but notes that the affected joint becomes very tender and red during these episodes. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint with visible tophi on the ear helix. Serum uric acid level is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to fatigue and joint pain. He reports experiencing stiffness in his hands and knees, especially in the morning, lasting for more than an hour. He has also noted swelling in these joints and difficulty performing daily activities. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and both knees. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 75 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He describes the pain as being concentrated in his hands and wrists bilaterally. He also reports feeling fatigued and has experienced some unintended weight loss. He denies any recent infections or new medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. ESR and CRP are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressively worsening deformities in his hands over the past several years. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. His medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. X-rays of the hands show erosions and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.D: Refer to rheumatology for disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) management

question: A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of swelling and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. He denies any recent trauma or new medications. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. An x-ray of the hands shows soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-year history of recurrent episodes of finger pain and color changes. He describes the fingers turning white and then blue when exposed to cold, followed by redness and tingling upon rewarming. Recently, he has noted progressive thickening and tightening of the skin on his fingers, which has made it difficult for him to flex or extend them fully. He also reports diffuse joint pain and occasional heartburn but denies any significant weight changes or fatigue. On examination, the fingers appear shiny and taut, particularly over the distal phalanges. Laboratory tests reveal a negative rheumatoid factor, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and positive antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain in both hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting about two hours before easing somewhat as the day progresses. He has noticed swelling in his fingers and describes the pain as a dull ache. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any significant family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this condition?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He notes that the symptoms are more severe in the morning, often lasting more than an hour. He also describes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, primarily affecting the small joints of his hands and wrists. He denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and elevated C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to joint pain that has been migrating over the past week. Initially, he complained of pain in his knees, which then moved to his elbows and wrists. His symptoms began a few weeks after he had a sore throat that resolved without treatment. On examination, his joints are warm and tender, but there is no significant swelling. He has a temperature of 38.3°C (100.9°F). Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). His antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening joint pain and deformities in his hands. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty with daily activities due to finger swelling and pain. His medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He denies smoking and consumes alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints, as well as boutonniere deformities of several fingers. Laboratory studies show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He reports that the pain is symmetric, affecting his wrists, knees, and ankles for the past 3 months. The stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting over an hour before he can comfortably move. He denies any recent infections, trauma, or fevers. He has a history of hypertension controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is evidence of swollen and tender joints, with reduced range of motion in the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, a normal mean corpuscular volume, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of chronic lower back pain. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. He denies any significant joint swelling or redness, but notes occasional stiffness in his neck and lower back that limits his mobility, particularly in the morning. He has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen intermittently for pain. On examination, there is reduced spinal flexion and tenderness along the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with acute pain and swelling in his left knee. He reports that the symptoms started suddenly after playing a vigorous game of basketball the previous day. He denies any significant history of joint pain or swelling in the past. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On examination, the left knee is swollen, warm, and tender to touch, with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no redness or other systemic symptoms. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Synovial fluid analysis reveals a WBC count of 25,000/mm³ with a predominance of neutrophils. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. He describes the pain as symmetric, involving both his wrists and knees, and notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour. His past medical history is unremarkable. He does not take any medications regularly. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 11 g/dL
Hematocrit 34%
Mean corpuscular volume 88 fL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue, persistent nasal congestion, and a cough that has not improved with over-the-counter medication. He reports occasional hemoptysis and unintended weight loss of 5 kg over the past few months. He also describes joint pain, especially in the mornings, which improves throughout the day. His past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sinuses and crackles in the lung bases. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine levels and positive c-ANCA. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Urinalysis demonstrates hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and weakness in his right foot that has progressively worsened over the past few weeks. He reports tripping frequently and having difficulty lifting his foot when walking. He has a history of controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. The patient denies any trauma or recent infections. On examination, there is noticeable weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot, and a high-stepping gait is observed. Sensation is intact, and reflexes are normal in both lower limbs. There is no significant joint pain or swelling. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. MRI of the spine is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic sinusitis, persistent cough, and hemoptysis over the last 3 months. He reports increasing fatigue and unintentional weight loss. He also notes mild joint pain in his knees and wrists. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril.
