On examination, there is tenderness over the maxillary sinuses, bilateral crackles on lung auscultation, and mild edema of the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine and a positive c-ANCA. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic because of joint pain and stiffness. He notes the stiffness is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. He reports swelling and pain in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. He has a family history of autoimmune disease. He is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. On examination, his joints are warm, swollen, and tender with decreased grip strength. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the last three months. He describes a persistent, gradual weakness in his lower extremities, accompanied by occasional numbness and tingling. He denies any recent trauma or significant pain in his joints but reports generalized fatigue. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension. On examination, the patient exhibits decreased strength in the lower limbs, with muscle tenderness and mild atrophy noted in the quadriceps. Reflexes are diminished in the legs. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and mild anemia. Electromyography reveals decreased motor unit potentials. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. He describes the pain as persistent and diffuse, affecting his shoulders, back, and hips. He reports waking up feeling unrefreshed and struggles to concentrate at work due to his fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there are no swollen or tender joints, and he appears anxious. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain that has gradually worsened. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and states that his fingers feel swollen. He also reports noticing firm, non-tender lumps under the skin over his elbows. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists, with subcutaneous nodules noted on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever accompanied by night sweats and significant weight loss over the past three months. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. The patient reports some joint pain and fatigue but denies any morning stiffness or specific joint swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. A lymph node biopsy is performed as seen in Figure A. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of progressive joint pain and deformity. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and swelling in both his hands and wrists. He also complains of occasional fever and fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is visible swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). An X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the emergency department with complaints of discoloration of the skin in a net-like pattern on his legs and recurrent episodes of leg pain and swelling. He reports having multiple episodes of deep vein thrombosis over the past year, for which he has been on anticoagulation therapy. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities. Laboratory investigations show elevated inflammatory markers and positive anti-cardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in both hands for the past six months. He describes the stiffness as being most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving somewhat throughout the day. He denies any recent trauma or new physical activities. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. His laboratory results are significant for a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient?

A 53-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue, easy bruising, and joint pain primarily affecting his knees and elbows. He also reports feeling more tired than usual and having occasional headaches. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctiva and scattered petechiae on his lower extremities. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 8.2 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,300/mm^3, and platelets of 70,000/mm^3. Bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity with fatty infiltration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with three months of worsening stiffness and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports difficulty with his daily activities, especially in the morning when the stiffness lasts for about two hours. His past medical history is significant for mild hypertension managed with a thiazide diuretic. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a C-reactive protein level of 22 mg/L (normal <10 mg/L) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/h. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty using his hands, especially in the mornings. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma. His temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old man presents to the clinic with low back pain that has persisted for the past 6 months. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. He denies any significant trauma or injury to the back. He also reports occasional pain in his hips and difficulty sleeping due to discomfort. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications regularly. He does not smoke or use illicit drugs and occasionally drinks alcohol. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to his physician with complaints of fatigue, night sweats, and an unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the last two months. He also reports persistent lymphadenopathy, which he first noticed in his neck and now feels similar swellings in his groin. Additionally, he describes mild joint discomfort in his knees and wrists but denies significant morning stiffness. His past medical history is notable for well-controlled hyperlipidemia. On examination, he has palpable lymph nodes in the cervical and inguinal regions, but no hepatosplenomegaly is noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a recent diagnosis of hypertension. He reports new-onset fatigue and occasional headaches over the past month. His medical history is unremarkable except for mild osteoarthritis managed with over-the-counter medication. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or significant weight changes. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 168/102 mm Hg, and there is mild bilateral ankle edema. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, and urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria with no casts. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows asymmetrical renal size with the right kidney being smaller. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and scaly skin lesions. He reports that over the last few months, he has experienced pain in the joints of his fingers and toes, which occurs mainly in the morning and improves throughout the day. He also mentions a recent rash affecting his elbows and knees. His medical history reveals chronic plaque psoriasis, for which he uses topical treatments. On physical examination, there is asymmetric swelling in the distal interphalangeal joints of the right hand and toes, along with erythematous plaques covered by silvery scales on the extensor surfaces. Blood tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue. He mentions that these symptoms started about a week ago after recovering from a sore throat and fever, which he attributes to a viral illness. He states the body aches are particularly bad in the morning but improve somewhat as the day progresses. He denies any joint swelling or redness. On examination, he has tenderness to palpation over multiple muscle groups but no joint deformities. Laboratory findings show a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with fatigue and significant weight loss over the past 3 months. He also reports occasional dizziness and episodes of palpitations. He denies any recent changes in his diet or lifestyle. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals pallor and slight tachycardia. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 75 fL, and a ferritin level of 10 ng/mL. Further evaluation with a peripheral blood smear shows microcytic hypochromic red blood cells. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Check serum B12 and folate levels

question: A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care provider with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain. He describes the joint pain as a symmetric polyarthritis affecting his wrists and small joints of his hands for the last 3 months, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. On physical exam, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 88 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 75 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male presents to his primary care clinic with fatigue and pain in his joints. He complains of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and symmetric swelling of the joints in his hands and wrists for the past six months. He has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. He does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals tender and swollen metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 11.0 g/dL, a normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/h, and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive hand deformities and morning stiffness lasting over an hour for the past 8 months. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. He reports difficulty performing daily tasks due to pain and swelling in his hands. On examination, there is ulnar deviation of the fingers and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory results reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a persistent fever for the past three weeks. He describes intermittent chills and night sweats, accompanied by fatigue and muscle aches. He reports mild joint pain, predominantly in his wrists and knees, but denies any significant swelling. He has been feeling unusually tired and mentions losing about 5 pounds unintentionally over the last month. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, his temperature is 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a one-week history of purple, lace-like discoloration on his legs and recurrent episodes of leg swelling and pain. He reports two prior deep vein thromboses in the past year, for which he was treated with anticoagulation. He denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness, but does mention intermittent headaches and fatigue. He has no known allergies and does not take any regular medications aside from warfarin. Family history is significant for a brother who had a stroke at age 30. On examination, his vital signs are stable, but there is notable livedo reticularis over the lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count, normal renal and liver function tests, and an elevated D-dimer level. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is negative, and rheumatoid factor is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and mild joint pain in his wrists and ankles for the past two weeks. He reports feeling generally fatigued and has had a low-grade fever. He has no known allergies and denies any recent infections or travel. He works as a bartender and occasionally drinks alcohol. On examination, there are multiple tender, erythematous subcutaneous nodules over the anterior surfaces of his lower legs. Laboratory studies show mild leukocytosis and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness. He reports that for the past 5 months he has been experiencing pain in his wrists, fingers, and knees, which is worst in the morning and lasts for about 1-2 hours. He also notes fatigue and occasional fever. He has a family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP, and the presence of anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). X-ray of the hands reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of persistent fever and generalized fatigue. He reports night sweats and a significant weight loss of 10 pounds during this period. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any regular medications. On physical examination, there is mild tenderness over the abdomen, but no specific organomegaly. Laboratory findings show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures, chest X-ray, and basic infectious workup are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Perform a positron emission tomography (PET) scan
options.D: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness lasting for several months. He describes the pain as affecting his hands and wrists symmetrically, particularly in the morning, with stiffness lasting over an hour. He notes swelling in his fingers and difficulty performing tasks requiring fine motor skills. His medical history is notable for intermittent episodes of fatigue and low-grade fever. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, but no erythema. His vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show soft tissue swelling and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with a three-month history of bilateral hand and wrist swelling and stiffness, particularly in the morning lasting more than an hour. He also reports fatigue and a low-grade fever. He denies any trauma or overuse. On examination, there is evidence of synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are both positive. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 60-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that increases with activity and improves with rest. He denies any morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes. The patient has a medical history of obesity and mild hypertension controlled with a thiazide diuretic. He works as a construction manager and spends long hours on his feet. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the greater trochanter and pain with internal rotation of the hips. His gait is slightly antalgic. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers, and his rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and difficulty concentrating over the past 6 months. He reports that even after a full night's sleep, he does not feel rested and experiences mild joint pain, especially in the mornings. He denies any significant weight changes or fever. His past medical history includes seasonal allergies treated with antihistamines. On physical examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. There is no joint swelling or tenderness, and his neurological exam is normal. Laboratory tests reveal a thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level of 8.5 mIU/L (normal range: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and low free thyroxine (T4). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Order an MRI of the brain

question:
A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 3 months, along with progressive periorbital edema. He also reports morning joint stiffness in his wrists and ankles that improves after about an hour of activity. He denies any significant changes in diet or activity level. His medical history is non-contributory, and he has no known allergies. Blood pressure is 142/88 mm Hg, and physical examination reveals pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show:
Serum 
Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL
Albumin 2.4 g/dL
Urinalysis
Protein 3+
Microscopy
Fatty casts numerous
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and swelling, primarily in his wrists and knees. He also reports a rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure. He denies any significant medical history and does not take any medications. He works as an accountant and spends weekends outdoors hiking. On examination, he has a symmetrical arthritis involving the wrists and knees. There is a well-demarcated, erythematous rash across the bridge of his nose and cheeks. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive antinuclear antibody (ANA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and a rash. He reports spending a weekend hiking in a forested area and noticed a tick bite on his leg about three weeks ago. Since then, he has developed migratory joint pains and an erythematous rash that initially started on his thigh but has now spread to his trunk. He denies any fever, weight loss, or night sweats. On examination, there is an annular rash with a central clearing on his trunk and mild swelling in several joints including the knees and wrists. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with slowly progressive difficulty walking over the past eight months. He reports weakness and occasional cramping in his lower extremities, which he first noticed while hiking. He denies any numbness or tingling. His family history is unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On physical examination, he has bilateral lower extremity weakness, especially in the proximal muscles, and decreased deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase and aldolase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his hands and knees over the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and feels fatigued throughout the day. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well controlled with lisinopril. He denies any recent infections or injuries. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), elevated C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. X-rays of the hands show marginal erosions and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of progressive joint pain, swelling, and deformity in both hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and significant fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and he has not experienced trauma or recent infections. On physical examination, there is noticeable ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints, and tenderness over the wrist joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following characteristics is most associated with this patient's condition?

A 50-year-old male visits the rheumatology clinic with complaints of persistent pain in both hands and wrists for the past 18 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting approximately two hours that eases with movement. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Subcutaneous nodules are noted over the elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a fall on his outstretched hand while playing basketball. He reports immediate pain in his right wrist and is unable to move it due to severe discomfort. He denies any previous injuries to the area. His medical history is significant for occasional joint pain in his hands and morning stiffness that resolves within an hour. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness over the distal radius. An X-ray of the wrist reveals a displaced fracture of the distal radius. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management for this patient?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a two-week history of painful red nodules on his shins and joint pain in his ankles and knees. He reports feeling fatigued and has had a low-grade fever. He denies any history of trauma or travel. On further questioning, he mentions a recent sore throat that resolved without antibiotics. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. His family history is unremarkable. On physical examination, several erythematous, tender nodules are noted on the anterior aspects of both lower legs. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. A chest x-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness. He reports that for the past six months, he has experienced pain and swelling in his hands and wrists, especially in the morning. The stiffness lasts for about two hours and gradually improves with activity. He denies any significant weight loss, fever, or skin rash. His medical history is notable for eczema, which he manages with topical treatments. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male visits the clinic due to ongoing bilateral hand and wrist pain over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as writing and opening jars. He also notes the presence of small, painless bumps on his elbows. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A hand x-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports morning stiffness in his hands and wrists lasting more than an hour. His medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled with medication. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A rheumatoid factor test is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue, low-grade fever, and multiple tender nodules on his forearms and elbows that have developed over the past few weeks. He also reports experiencing joint pain and morning stiffness lasting around 30 minutes daily. His medical history is significant for smoking and hypertension. On examination, firm subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of his forearms. Blood tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past three months. He reports feeling unusually tired, even after a full night's sleep, and has lost approximately 15 pounds during this period. His medical history is unremarkable aside from mild hypertension controlled with lisinopril. He denies alcohol use and has a 20-pack-year smoking history, having quit five years ago. On examination, he appears pale. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 78 fL, and ferritin of 12 ng/mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists. He notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before he feels any relief. He has also noticed swelling and warmth in the affected joints. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional heartburn, for which he takes over-the-counter antacids. He does not smoke and consumes alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of pain and swelling in both wrists and hands for the past 6 months. He reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning, with stiffness lasting for about 1 to 2 hours, and improve as the day progresses. He denies any recent trauma or injuries and reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fever. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of worsening pain and deformity in his fingers over the past year. He mentions that morning stiffness lasts more than an hour and improves with activity. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, treated with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints with ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic with joint pain and swelling. He reports having red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees, which appeared several months before the joint symptoms began. The joint pain is primarily in his right knee and left wrist and is not symmetric. He denies any morning stiffness but notices the pain worsens after physical activity. On examination, there are thick, silvery plaques with well-defined edges on the extensor surfaces. The right knee appears swollen with warmth and tenderness, while the left wrist shows signs of mild synovitis. There is no notable nail pitting. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful oral ulcers and recent genital sores for the past three months. He notes that these episodes tend to occur every few weeks and are associated with fatigue. He has also experienced intermittent joint pain, particularly in his knees and ankles, but no significant swelling. He denies any recent travel, sexually transmitted infections, or similar symptoms in the past. On examination, there are shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and erythematous sores on the genitals. No synovial swelling is noted. Laboratory workup shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician by his mother due to joint pain and swelling. Two weeks ago, he experienced a sore throat and fever, which resolved spontaneously. Now, he reports migrating pain and swelling in his knees, elbows, and wrists. He also mentions feeling fatigued. On examination, there is warmth, redness, and swelling in the affected joints. He has no significant past medical history. His temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists lasting more than an hour each morning for the past six months. He reports increased fatigue and difficulty performing daily tasks that require hand dexterity. His past medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man comes to the clinic with a complaint of recurrent mouth ulcers that have been present for the past few months. He also reports painful genital sores that appeared around the same time. The patient mentions occasional joint pain, which he attributes to a recent increase in his exercise regimen. He denies fever, weight loss, or recent travel. On examination, multiple aphthous ulcers are noted in the oral cavity, and there are healing shallow ulcerations on the genitalia. No synovitis is observed in the joints. Basic laboratory tests, including blood counts, liver function tests, and inflammatory markers, are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with chronic sinus issues, including nasal congestion and recurrent sinusitis. He has been experiencing a persistent cough and exertional dyspnea over the past few months. On further questioning, he reports unintended weight loss and fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any significant family history of autoimmune diseases. Vital signs are stable, and physical examination shows no significant abnormalities except for mild nasal crusting and tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and the presence of red cell casts in the urine. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain in both hands, wrists, and knees for the last few months. He describes the pain as more pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving slightly as the day progresses. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, and he takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with some warmth noted. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and progressive deformity in his hands over the past three years. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing tasks that require manual dexterity, such as buttoning his shirt. He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed five years ago and has not been compliant with his medication regimen due to side effects. On physical examination, there are ulnar deviations of the fingers, subluxations at the wrists, and several swan-neck deformities. Laboratory tests reveal a high rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?
